topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT611 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fundamental Definition of Reliability

**Reliability** is formally defined as the *probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under specified operating conditions (environment, load, stress, temperature) for a specified period of time*.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = Reliability function at time $t$
- $T$ = Random variable representing the time-to-failure
- $t$ = Specified mission time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Reliability is fundamentally a **probability**, so it is a **dimensionless quantity** bounded between **0 and 1** (or 0% to 100%). It is *time-dependent* — a component's reliability at $t = 100$ hours will always be less than or equal to its reliability at $t = 50$ hours.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of reliability like the **"on-time performance" of a daily train**. Imagine a commuter train that promises to run every day for a year (8760 hours). If the train operates without failure on 8000 of those days and breaks down on 760 days, its reliability over one year is approximately **8000 / 8760 ≈ 0.913**.

Now imagine the railway authority asks: *"What is the probability that the train will still be running fine after, say, 6 months of continuous service?"* This probability — calculated across thousands of identical trains under identical conditions — is what engineers call **reliability at time $t$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: Reliability is *not* about whether something CAN fail (everything fails eventually). It is about the *probability* that failure has NOT occurred within a specified mission duration.

### 1.3 Related Core Definitions (KTU Module 1 Vocabulary Bank)

| Term | Formal Definition | Symbol/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | Probability that a system is operational at a given instant of time | $A(t)$ (dimensionless) |
| **Maintainability** | Probability that a failed system will be restored to operational condition within a given time | $M(t)$ (dimensionless) |
| **Failure** | Termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function | — |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | Expected (average) time until first failure for non-repairable items | Hours |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | Expected time between consecutive failures for repairable items | Hours |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | Average time required to restore a failed system | Hours |
| **Failure Rate** | Conditional probability of failure per unit time, given survival up to time $t$ | $\lambda(t)$ (failures/hour) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTBF ≠ MTTF**: MTTF applies to **non-repairable** components (e.g., resistors, capacitors, light bulbs). MTBF applies to **repairable** systems (e.g., cars, aircraft, machines). For KTU board exams, this distinction is a *favourite 2-mark trap question*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Unpacking the Definition — The Three Pillars

Reliability engineering's definition rests on three non-negotiable pillars. Missing any one invalidates the engineering analysis:

1. **Specified Function** — What the item is supposed to do. A pump must deliver $Q$ m³/s at $H$ meters head.
2. **Specified Operating Conditions** — Environment, load, temperature, humidity, vibration profile. A satellite component operates under vacuum and radiation; a marine component operates under salt corrosion.
3. **Specified Period of Time** — The *mission time*. A missile battery only needs to last 30 seconds; a power plant turbine needs to last 30 years.

### 2.2 Reliability as a Complement of Failure Probability

Since a component is either *working* or *failed* (binary state) at any time $t$, the probabilities of these two events must sum to unity:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

Where $F(t)$ is the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of failure, also called the **Unreliability function**:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - R(t)$$

The corresponding **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of time-to-failure is obtained by differentiating:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Probability of survival past time $t$ |
| **Unreliability** | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Cumulative failure probability |
| **Failure Density** | $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous failure probability density |
| **Failure Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional failure rate (hazard function) |
| **Exponential Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate model |
| **Mean Life** | $\theta = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | Expected time-to-failure |
| **Exponential Mean Life** | $\theta = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For constant failure rate |
| **Steady-State Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational fraction (long-run) |
| **Inherent Availability** | $A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Excludes logistics/downtime |
| **Mission Reliability** | $R_M$ | Probability of completing the mission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exponential Distribution Connection**: When $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), the exponential distribution naturally emerges as $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This is the workhorse model for electronic components and is the **default assumption in most KTU numerical problems**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Reliability Target | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | $R \geq 0.9999999$ ("seven nines") per flight hour | Catastrophic human/asset loss risk |
| **Consumer Electronics** | $R \approx 0.95$ over 2 years | Cost-driven market competition |
| **Medical Implants (Pacemaker)** | $R \geq 0.99$ over 7 years | Life-critical, non-serviceable |
| **Nuclear Power Plants** | $R \geq 0.99999$ per year | Public safety & regulatory mandate |
| **Defence Missiles** | Mission Reliability $\geq 0.90$ | One-shot, no repair possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **higher the consequence of failure**, the **higher the reliability target** demanded by the design specification. Reliability engineering is fundamentally about *balancing safety, performance, and cost*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Failure Rate to Reliability Function

We begin with the **conditional probability** definition of failure rate (also called the *hazard function* $h(t)$):

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 1**: Apply the conditional probability identity.

By definition, $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$. Since the event $\{t < T \leq t + \Delta t\}$ is a *subset* of $\{T > t\}$ (because the component survived to $t$), we have:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute this back into the failure rate definition.

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

**Step 3**: Recognize the numerator as the derivative of $F(t)$.

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to reliability form using $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ so that $\frac{dF}{dt} = -\frac{dR}{dt}$.

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5**: Rearrange as a first-order separable ODE.

$$R(t) \cdot \lambda(t) \, dt = -dR(t)$$

**Step 6**: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new component has 100% reliability at $t = 0$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(\tau)} \, dR(\tau) \\
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= -\left[ \ln R(\tau) \right]_{0}^{t} \\
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) \\
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= -\ln R(t) \quad (\text{since } \ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7**: Solve for $R(t)$.

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau \right)}$$

This is the **general reliability function**, valid for *any* failure rate profile $\lambda(t)$.

**Step 8**: Specialize to the **constant failure rate** case ($\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{const}$).

$$\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, d\tau \right) \\
R(t) &= \exp(-\lambda t) = e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Exponential Reliability Model**, the cornerstone of reliability block diagrams and series/parallel system analysis at the KTU level.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for the Exponential Model

**Step 1**: Start from the definition of expected value.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2**: For the exponential model, $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3**: Integrate by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt \\
&= [0 - 0] + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \\
&= \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut**: For any exponential-model question, the KTU board examiners expect you to *immediately* recognize that mean life $= 1/\lambda$ and reliability at time $t$ is $e^{-\lambda t}$. Do not re-derive unless specifically asked.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem Context (Typical KTU 2-mark type)**: A batch of resistors has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures per hour.

| Quantity | Computation | Numerical Result |
|---|---|---|
| Reliability at $t = 100$ hr | $e^{-0.001 \times 100}$ | $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$ |
| Reliability at $t = 1000$ hr | $e^{-0.001 \times 1000}$ | $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ |
| MTTF | $1 / 0.001$ | $1000$ hours |
| Unreliability at 1000 hr | $1 - e^{-1}$ | $0.6321$ |
| Failure rate at any $t$ | constant | $0.001$ /hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive result**: At $t = \text{MTTF}$, $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$. This means *only ~37% of components survive beyond their mean life*. The median life ($R = 0.5$) is $t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx 0.693 \cdot \text{MTTF}$. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reliability Engineering Conceptual Map (Module 1)

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the definition of reliability connects to its derived metrics and engineering applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reliability Definition] --> B[Three Pillars]
    B --> B1[Specified Function]
    B --> B2[Specified Conditions]
    B --> B3[Specified Time]
    A --> C[Core Mathematical Forms]
    C --> C1[R of t = P of T greater than t]
    C --> C2[F of t = 1 minus R of t]
    C --> C3[f of t = derivative of F of t]
    A --> D[Failure Rate]
    D --> D1[Lambda of t = f of t divided by R of t]
    D --> D2[Constant Lambda]
    D2 --> E[Exponential Model]
    E --> E1[R of t = exp of minus lambda t]
    E --> E2[MTTF = 1 divided by lambda]
    A --> F[Engineering Applications]
    F --> F1[Aerospace]
    F --> F2[Nuclear]
    F --> F3[Medical Devices]
    F --> F4[Consumer Electronics]
    A --> G[Availability Maintainability]
    G --> G1[A = MTBF divided by MTBF plus MTTR]
    G --> G2[M of t = repair probability]
```

### 4.2 Reliability Function and Failure Density Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Component Starts Operating] --> Survive[Survives to time t]
    Survive --> Prob[R of t = Probability]
    Prob --> After{Time passes}
    After --> Yes[Continues to function]
    After --> No[Failure occurs]
    No --> FailDist[f of t : Failure density]
    FailDist --> RateCalc[Lambda of t = f divided by R]
    RateCalc --> ConstRate[Constant Lambda leads to Exponential R of t]
```

### 4.3 Mission Time vs. Reliability Trade-off (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Component under stress] --> StressCheck{Conditions specified?}
    StressCheck -- Yes --> TimeCheck{Mission time t chosen?}
    StressCheck -- No --> X[Invalid analysis]
    TimeCheck -- Yes --> RCompute[Compute R of t]
    TimeCheck -- No --> Y[Cannot compute reliability]
    RCompute --> Output[Reliability value between 0 and 1]
    RCompute --> MTTFOut[Mean life computed]
    Output --> Decision{Acceptable for design?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Deploy[Deploy system]
    Decision -- No --> Redesign[Add redundancy or improve component]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU**: When asked to *illustrate* the relationship between $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)$, and $\lambda(t)$ in the exam, draw a simple block diagram similar to the architecture above. Always label the **direction of derivation** with arrows — examiners reward explicit visualization of mathematical interconnections.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

**Q1.** Define *Reliability* and *Maintainability*. State clearly how these two terms differ in their scope.

**Model Answer**:
**Reliability** is the probability that an item will perform its required function under stated conditions for a stated period of time.
**Maintainability** is the probability that a failed item will be restored to an operable condition within a given period of time when maintenance action is performed in accordance with prescribed procedures.

The key difference: *Reliability* pertains to the **probability of not failing**, while *Maintainability* pertains to the **probability of being repaired within a time limit after failure has occurred*. The former is a preventive metric; the latter is a restorative metric.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**Q2.** A system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour. Calculate its reliability for a mission time of $t = 500$ hours. Also compute its MTTF.

**Model Answer**:
Using the exponential reliability model: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$

$$R(500) = e^{-0.0005 \times 500} = e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

Hence, the system reliability at 500 hours is approximately **0.7788**, and the mean time to failure is **2000 hours**.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 1, End Semester Pattern]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Define Reliability as a probability function. Starting from the conditional probability definition of failure rate $\lambda(t)$, derive the general expression for $R(t)$ in terms of $\lambda(t)$.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The conditional failure rate is defined as:
$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

Using the identity $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ and the fact that the failure event lies within the survival event:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $t$ with boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln R(t)$$

$$\therefore \quad R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)$$

For constant $\lambda$: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**(b)** *For a complex repairable system, the recorded uptimes and downtimes during a one-year test are as follows: 7 failures occurred, total uptime = 7200 hours, total downtime = 1560 hours. Calculate (i) MTBF, (ii) MTTR, (iii) Inherent Availability, and (iv) Reliability for a 200-hour mission assuming constant failure rate.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

Given: Total uptime $T_u = 7200$ hr, Total downtime $T_d = 1560$ hr, Number of failures $N = 7$.

**(i) MTBF** = Total uptime / Number of failures
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{7200}{7} \approx 1028.57 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTTR** = Total downtime / Number of failures
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1560}{7} \approx 222.86 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Inherent Availability** $A_i$ = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
$$A_i = \frac{1028.57}{1028.57 + 222.86} = \frac{1028.57}{1251.43} \approx 0.8219$$

**(iv) Failure rate** $\lambda$ = 1 / MTBF (constant rate assumption)
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{1028.57} \approx 9.723 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

Reliability for 200-hour mission:
$$R(200) = e^{-\lambda \times 200} = e^{-9.723 \times 10^{-4} \times 200} = e^{-0.1945} \approx 0.8233$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Define the following terms with one example each: (i) MTTF, (ii) MTBF, (iii) Failure Rate, (iv) Mission Reliability.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(i) MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**: The expected time to failure for a *non-repairable* item. *Example*: A capacitor rated at 50,000 hours MTTF means on average it will operate for 50,000 hours before failing (open or short circuit).

**(ii) MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: The expected time between successive failures for a *repairable* system. *Example*: An aircraft engine with MTBF = 2000 flight hours means on average 2000 hours elapse between consecutive failures.

**(iii) Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$**: The conditional probability per unit time that a unit, having survived to time $t$, will fail in the next infinitesimal interval. Measured in failures/hour or %/1000 hours (also called FIT — Failures In Time). *Example*: Electronic components typically have $\lambda \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ per hour.

**(iv) Mission Reliability $R_M$**: The probability that a system will perform its required function for the *specified mission duration* without failure. *Example*: A missile has mission reliability $R_M \geq 0.90$ for a 30-second flight time.

**(b)** *A radar system has an MTBF of 500 hours and an MTTR of 10 hours. If the system operates continuously, calculate (i) the steady-state availability, (ii) the expected downtime in a 30-day month, and (iii) the reliability for a 100-hour continuous mission assuming exponential failure distribution.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

Given: MTBF = 500 hr, MTTR = 10 hr, Mission time $t = 100$ hr, Period $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hr.

**(i) Steady-State Availability**:
$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{500}{500 + 10} = \frac{500}{510} \approx 0.9804$$

**(ii) Expected Downtime** per month:
$$T_d = T \times (1 - A) = 720 \times (1 - 0.9804) = 720 \times 0.0196 \approx 14.12 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability for 100-hour mission**:
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ per hour}$$

$$R(100) = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** (validated against KTU examiner reports):
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks)**: Writing MTTF as "hours/failure" instead of "hours". MTTF is a *time*, not a *rate*.
> 2. **Mixing MTTF and MTBF (–2 marks)**: Using MTBF formula for a non-repairable bulb problem. Always check if the item is repairable.
> 3. **Forgetting the *Exponential* Assumption (–1 mark)**: Applying $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ when the question does not state constant failure rate. If not stated, write "assuming constant $\lambda$" explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping Boundary Condition (–1 mark)**: In derivation questions, not mentioning $R(0) = 1$ during integration — examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **Wrong Availability Formula (–2 marks)**: Using instantaneous availability formula in a long-run problem. Use steady-state $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ for long-run questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📋 KTU Module 1 — Definition Quick Revision Checklist**

- **Reliability** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — probability of survival past time $t$ under stated conditions.
- **Unreliability** $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ — cumulative failure probability.
- **Failure Density** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ — instantaneous rate of failure occurrence.
- **Failure Rate** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — conditional rate given survival to $t$.
- **Exponential Model** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — applies when $\lambda$ is constant.
- **MTTF** = $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (non-repairable), **MTBF** = $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (repairable, but used differently).
- **Availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — long-run operational fraction.
- **Maintainability** $M(t)$ — repair probability within time $t$.
- Always **state the assumed distribution** (usually exponential) explicitly in numerical answers.
- Always **mention boundary condition** $R(0) = 1$ in derivation questions.
- Always **convert** all time units to a *single consistent unit* (e.g., all hours) before computing.
- Remember: **$R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$** — only ~37% survive past the mean life.
- **Median life** $= \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx 0.693 \cdot \text{MTTF}$.
- **Mission reliability** refers to a *specified* time, not "forever".
- **Inherent availability** excludes external logistics delay; **Achieved availability** includes it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT611 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Reliability Engineering (OESFT611)**, the **Probability Concept** forms the axiomatic foundation upon which all reliability metrics, failure rate models, and statistical inference procedures are built. The KTU 2024 scheme defines probability as a measure of the likelihood of occurrence of a random event, expressed as a numerical value between **0** and **1** (inclusive), where:

- **0** represents an *impossible event*.
- **1** represents a *certain event*.
- Any value in between represents the degree of *uncertainty* or *likelihood*.

Mathematically, for a sample space $S$ and an event $A \subseteq S$, the probability of $A$ is denoted $P(A)$, and must satisfy the **Kolmogorov Axioms (1933)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Probability is a real-valued function $P$ defined on a collection of events of a sample space, satisfying the three Kolmogorov axioms: (i) $P(A) \ge 0$ for every event $A$, (ii) $P(S) = 1$, and (iii) $P(A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \dots) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$ for mutually exclusive events."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spare Parts Box" Intuition

Imagine you are an industrial maintenance engineer in a KTU-affiliated Kerala manufacturing plant producing PCB assemblies. You open a large bin containing **1000 electrical relays**, of which **15 are defective** (a typical industrial defect rate of **1.5%**).

Now, you are asked: *"What is the likelihood that the very next relay you pick up is defective?"*

Since the bin has **15 defective units out of 1000**, your intuitive answer is **15/1000 = 0.015** (or 1.5%). This intuitive fraction is, in its essence, the **classical definition of probability**:

$$P(\text{Defective Relay}) = \frac{\text{Number of Favorable Outcomes}}{\text{Total Number of Equally Likely Outcomes}} = \frac{15}{1000} = 0.015$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Reliability Engineering:** Every reliability metric — whether it is the **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**, the **Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$**, or the **System Availability $A(t)$** — is fundamentally a probability statement. When we say a component has a reliability of $R(t) = 0.95$ at $t = 1000$ hours, we are making a probability assertion that the component will survive (i.e., the favorable event "survival" will occur) under specified operating conditions.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terms — The Building Blocks

| Term | Notation | Plain English Meaning |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| **Random Experiment** | $E$ | An experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty (e.g., life of a capacitor). |
| **Sample Space** | $S$ or $\Omega$ | The set of **all possible outcomes** of a random experiment. |
| **Event** | $A$ | Any subset of the sample space, including the empty set $\emptyset$ and $S$ itself. |
| **Sure Event** | $S$ | An event that always occurs, with $P(S) = 1$. |
| **Impossible Event** | $\emptyset$ | An event that never occurs, with $P(\emptyset) = 0$. |
| **Mutually Exclusive Events** | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ | Two events that **cannot occur simultaneously**. |
| **Exhaustive Events** | $\bigcup_{i} A_i = S$ | A collection of events that covers the entire sample space. |
| **Complementary Event** | $A^c$ or $A'$ | The set of all outcomes in $S$ that are **not** in $A$. |
| **Independent Events** | $A \perp B$ | Occurrence of $A$ does not affect the occurrence of $B$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** Students frequently confuse *"mutually exclusive"* with *"independent"*. They are **not the same**. Mutually exclusive events have a **logical constraint** ($A \cap B = \emptyset$); independent events have a **probabilistic constraint** ($P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$). Two mutually exclusive events (with non-zero probabilities) are actually **dependent**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Definitions of Probability (Exam Favorite)

Probability can be defined in three mathematically distinct but complementary ways. The KTU board examiner frequently tests your ability to **identify which definition applies to a given problem**.

### 2.1.1 Classical (Theoretical / A Priori) Definition
Used when the sample space consists of **equally likely outcomes** (e.g., dice rolls, coin tosses, card draws).

$$P(A) = \frac{n(A)}{n(S)}$$

where $n(A)$ is the number of outcomes favorable to $A$, and $n(S)$ is the total number of outcomes in the sample space.

> **Limitation:** Cannot be applied when outcomes are **not equally likely** (e.g., probability that a randomly chosen semiconductor chip is defective in a production batch).

### 2.1.2 Empirical (Statistical / A Posteriori / Relative Frequency) Definition
Used when we conduct an experiment **$n$ times** and an event $A$ occurs $n_A$ times. As $n \to \infty$:

$$P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n_A}{n}$$

> **Application in Reliability:** When we have **field failure data** from a population of components (e.g., 5000 bearings in service, 73 of which failed within 1 year), the empirical probability of failure is $73/5000 = 0.0146$.

### 2.1.3 Axiomatic (Modern / Kolmogorov) Definition
The mathematically rigorous foundation used in modern reliability theory. A probability function $P$ is a mapping from events to real numbers satisfying three axioms:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \ge 0$ for all $A \subseteq S$.
2. **Normalization:** $P(S) = 1$.
3. **Countable Additivity:** If $A_1, A_2, \dots$ are mutually exclusive, then $P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$.

## 2.2 The Conditional Probability Concept (Critical for Reliability)

**Conditional probability** is the probability of an event $A$ occurring, **given that** another event $B$ has already occurred. This is the cornerstone of **Bayesian reliability analysis** and **fault tree analysis**.

For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Bayesian Reliability Updating:** Suppose a transformer has a prior failure probability of 0.02 based on manufacturer data. After performing an insulation resistance test (event $B$), we obtain a new (updated) failure probability $P(A \mid B)$. This *updating* of belief upon receipt of new evidence is precisely what **Bayes' Theorem** formalizes, and it is heavily used in **prognostics and health management (PHM)** of industrial systems.

## 2.3 The Multiplication Rule of Probability

Rearranging the conditional probability formula yields the **Multiplication Rule**:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

For **independent events** $A$ and $B$, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$, so:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

> **Reliability Application:** If two components in series are **independent**, the system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities: $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \dots R_n$.

## 2.4 The Law of Total Probability

If $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$ is a partition of the sample space $S$ (mutually exclusive and exhaustive), then for any event $A$:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$

## 2.5 Bayes' Theorem (The Inferential Engine)

For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(A)}$$

Using the Law of Total Probability in the denominator, for a partition $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$:

$$P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terminology Map (Memorize for KTU 14-mark questions):**
> - $P(B_i)$ → **Prior probability** (belief before observing $A$).
> - $P(A \mid B_i)$ → **Likelihood** (probability of evidence under hypothesis).
> - $P(B_i \mid A)$ → **Posterior probability** (updated belief after observing $A$).
> - $P(A)$ → **Marginal probability** (normalizing constant / evidence).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the *complete* probability toolkit required for Module 1. Print this and memorize before the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Name | Application in Reliability |
|---|---------|------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $0 \le P(A) \le 1$ | Boundedness Axiom | Bounds any reliability probability. |
| 2 | $P(S) = 1$ | Certain Event | At least one outcome must occur. |
| 3 | $P(A) + P(A^c) = 1$ | Complement Rule | Compute $P(\text{survival}) = 1 - P(\text{failure})$. |
| 4 | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Addition Rule (General) | Failure of parallel systems. |
| 5 | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | Addition Rule (Mutually Exclusive) | Disjoint failure modes. |
| 6 | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Multiplication Rule | Sequential dependent failures. |
| 7 | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Multiplication Rule (Independent) | Series system reliability. |
| 8 | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Conditional Probability | Failure given stress. |
| 9 | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | Total Probability | Aggregate failure from multiple causes. |
| 10 | $P(B_i \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)}{\sum_j P(A \mid B_j) P(B_j)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Posterior failure probability update. |
| 11 | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - P(A^c \cap B^c)$ | De Morgan's Form | Useful in survival analysis. |
| 12 | $n! \big/ \big[k!(n-k)!\big]$ | Combinations (Choose $k$ from $n$) | Counting equally likely reliability outcomes. |

> **Note on Symbols:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in the conditional probability formulas is the *conditioning* operator — it reads as "given that". In KTU 2024 textbooks and previous question papers, this is the *only* allowed meaning of $\vert$ in probability contexts. It is **not** the absolute value operator.

## 2.7 Venn Diagram Visualization (Mental Model)

A Venn diagram of two overlapping events $A$ and $B$ inside a sample space $S$ visualizes all the regions needed:

- Region $A \cap B$ (overlap): both events occur.
- Region $A \setminus B$ (only $A$): $A$ occurs, $B$ does not.
- Region $B \setminus A$ (only $B$): $B$ occurs, $A$ does not.
- Region outside both (in $S$ but not $A$ or $B$): neither occurs.

The probabilities of all four regions **must sum to 1**. This single fact — *the total probability is unity* — is the conceptual root of both the Addition Rule and the Law of Total Probability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive it?** KTU ESE examiners at the 14-mark level frequently award **2 bonus marks** to students who can *derive* Bayes' Theorem rather than merely state it. Below is the full derivation a board examiner expects.

### Step 1 — Start with the Symmetric Definition of Joint Probability

By the definition of conditional probability, the joint probability of $A$ and $B$ occurring can be written in **two symmetric ways**, since the intersection $A \cap B$ is the same set regardless of the order in which we condition:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 2 — Equate the Two Expressions

Since both (i) and (ii) describe the *same* set $A \cap B$, they must be equal:

$$P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

### Step 3 — Isolate $P(B \mid A)$

Assuming $P(A) > 0$ (so that division is valid), we divide both sides by $P(A)$:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Law of Total Probability in the Denominator

To make the formula computationally useful, we express the denominator $P(A)$ by conditioning on a partition $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$ of the sample space:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$

### Step 5 — Final Bayes' Theorem

Substituting Step 4 into Step 3:

$$\boxed{P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Reliability Engineering Scenario

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model KTU Problem Style:** A production line has two machines, M₁ and M₂, that produce the same component. M₁ produces **60%** of the output with a defect rate of **2%**, and M₂ produces **40%** of the output with a defect rate of **3%**. A randomly selected component is found to be defective. Find the probability that it came from machine M₁.

### Given Data

| Quantity | Value | Interpretation |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| $P(M_1)$ | $0.60$ | Prior probability — proportion from M₁ |
| $P(M_2)$ | $0.40$ | Prior probability — proportion from M₂ |
| $P(D \mid M_1)$ | $0.02$ | Likelihood — defective given from M₁ |
| $P(D \mid M_2)$ | $0.03$ | Likelihood — defective given from M₂ |
| Find | $P(M_1 \mid D)$ | Posterior probability — came from M₁ given defective |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Marginal Probability of Defect (Total Probability)**

$$P(D) = P(D \mid M_1) \cdot P(M_1) + P(D \mid M_2) \cdot P(M_2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$P(D) = (0.02)(0.60) + (0.03)(0.40)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term**

First term: $(0.02)(0.60) = 0.0120$

Second term: $(0.03)(0.40) = 0.0120$

**Step 4 — Sum the Terms**

$$P(D) = 0.0120 + 0.0120 = 0.0240$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid M_1) \cdot P(M_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{0.0120}{0.0240}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer**

$$\boxed{P(M_1 \mid D) = 0.5000 \text{ or } 50\%}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Even though M₁ produces 60% of all components, knowing that a component is defective *equalizes* the chance that it came from either machine (both 50%). This is because M₂ has a *proportionally higher* defect rate. This counter-intuitive result is the **base-rate fallacy** — and it has major implications in reliability engineering, particularly in **warranty claim attribution** and **root-cause failure analysis**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Series System Reliability Using Independence

> **Problem:** A system has three independent components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.90$. The system functions only if **all three** components function. Find the system reliability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recognize the System Topology**

This is a **series configuration**: all components must survive for the system to function.

**Step 2 — Apply the Multiplication Rule for Independent Events**

For independent components, the system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities:

$$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$R_s = (0.95)(0.98)(0.90)$$

**Step 4 — Compute in Sequential Order**

First multiplication: $(0.95)(0.98) = 0.9310$

Second multiplication: $(0.9310)(0.90) = 0.8379$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$\boxed{R_s = 0.8379 \approx 83.79\%}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the system reliability (0.8379) is **lower** than the *weakest* individual component reliability (0.90). This is a fundamental property of series systems — the **"weakest link"** effect. In a series system of $n$ independent components each with reliability $R$, the system reliability decays **exponentially** as $R^n$. This is why redundancy (parallel paths) is critical in safety-critical systems such as aircraft flight control systems and nuclear reactor shutdown systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario Clue in the Problem Statement | Use This Formula |
|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| "at least one of A or B occurs" | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ |
| "neither A nor B occurs" | $P(A^c \cap B^c) = 1 - P(A \cup B)$ |
| "A occurs given that B has occurred" | $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ |
| "components operate independently" | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ |
| "partition / multiple causes" | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ |
| "updated / posterior / new evidence" | Bayes' Theorem |
| "system fails if any component fails" | Series multiplication rule |
| "system works if at least one works" | Parallel formula: $1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i)$ |
| "exactly k out of n" | Binomial: $\binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Probability Concepts (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Probability Theory - Module 1"]
    root --> def1["Classical Definition"]
    root --> def2["Empirical Definition"]
    root --> def3["Axiomatic Definition Kolmogorov"]
    root --> cond["Conditional Probability P A given B"]
    root --> indep["Independent Events"]
    root --> total["Law of Total Probability"]
    root --> bayes["Bayes Theorem"]

    def2 --> n2["Application field failure data"]
    def3 --> n3["Non-negativity Normalization Additivity"]
    cond --> formula1["P A given B equals P A and B over P B"]
    indep --> formula2["P A and B equals P A times P B"]
    total --> formula3["P A equals sum over i of P A given Bi times P Bi"]
    bayes --> formula4["P Bi given A equals P A given Bi times P Bi over P A"]
    cond --> indep
    cond --> total
    total --> bayes
    indep --> n4["Application series system reliability Rs equals R1 times R2 times ... times Rn"]
    bayes --> n5["Application posterior reliability updating with new evidence"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Probability in Reliability Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Field Failure Data n observations"] --> preproc["Data Preprocessing Categorize events Identify sample space S"]
    preproc --> classical["Classical Probability Applicable only if equally likely outcomes"]
    preproc --> empirical["Empirical Probability P A equals nA divided by n"]
    preproc --> axiomatic["Axiomatic Probability Kolmogorov three axioms"]
    classical --> cond["Conditional Probability P A given B"]
    empirical --> cond
    axiomatic --> cond
    cond --> indep["Independence Check P A and B equals P A times P B"]
    cond --> total["Total Probability Law P A equals sum of P A given Bi times P Bi"]
    indep --> series["Series System Reliability Rs equals product of Ri"]
    total --> series
    total --> parallel["Parallel System Reliability Rs equals 1 minus product of 1 minus Ri"]
    series --> output["Final Reliability Metric R t or MTTF or lambda t"]
    parallel --> output
    cond --> bayes["Bayes Theorem Posterior update"]
    bayes --> output
```

## 4.3 Probability Tree Diagram Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Initial State Probability equals 1"]
    start --> b1["Branch B1 with prior P B1"]
    start --> b2["Branch B2 with prior P B2"]
    start --> b3["Branch B3 with prior P B3"]
    b1 --> a1["Outcome A with likelihood P A given B1"]
    b1 --> a2["Outcome A complement with likelihood 1 minus P A given B1"]
    b2 --> a3["Outcome A with likelihood P A given B2"]
    b2 --> a4["Outcome A complement with likelihood 1 minus P A given B2"]
    b3 --> a5["Outcome A with likelihood P A given B3"]
    b3 --> a6["Outcome A complement with likelihood 1 minus P A given B3"]
    a1 --> joint1["Joint P A and B1 equals P A given B1 times P B1"]
    a3 --> joint2["Joint P A and B2 equals P A given B2 times P B2"]
    a5 --> joint3["Joint P A and B3 equals P A given B3 times P B3"]
    joint1 --> marg["Marginal P A equals sum of all joint probabilities"]
    joint2 --> marg
    joint3 --> marg
    marg --> post["Posterior P Bi given A via Bayes formula"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree:** Each branch from the start node represents a **prior hypothesis** ($B_i$). Each branch from a hypothesis node represents a **possible outcome** (with its likelihood). The **leaf nodes** are the joint probabilities, and the **sum of all joint probabilities** reaching the same leaf type (e.g., all "A" leaves) gives the marginal probability of that outcome — which is precisely the Law of Total Probability applied visually.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State and explain the three axioms of probability as given by Kolmogorov.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The three Kolmogorov axioms, which form the axiomatic foundation of modern probability, are:

1. **Non-negativity Axiom (1 mark):** For any event $A$ in the sample space $S$, $P(A) \ge 0$. Probability cannot be negative.

2. **Normalization Axiom (1 mark):** The probability of the entire sample space is unity, $P(S) = 1$. At least one outcome in $S$ must occur with certainty.

3. **Countable Additivity Axiom (1 mark):** If $A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots$ are *countably many* mutually exclusive events, then $P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$. The probability of the union equals the sum of the individual probabilities when events do not overlap.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between mutually exclusive events and independent events with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Mutually exclusive events (1.5 marks):** Two events $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive if they cannot occur simultaneously, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$, so $P(A \cap B) = 0$. Example: When a single die is rolled, the events "getting a 3" and "getting a 5" are mutually exclusive.

**Independent events (1.5 marks):** Two events $A$ and $B$ are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the occurrence of the other, i.e., $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ and equivalently $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Example: Tossing two different coins — the outcome of the first coin does not influence the outcome of the second.

**Key Distinction:** Mutually exclusive events (with non-zero probabilities) are *always dependent*, not independent.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> - Students frequently **confuse prior and posterior** in Bayes' Theorem problems. Always identify which probability is given and which is sought **before writing the formula**. (Lose 2 marks.)
> - Students often **forget to verify independence** before applying the multiplication rule $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Always state the independence assumption explicitly. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - In series/parallel reliability problems, students **swap** the topology. If the system fails when *any* component fails → **series** (use product). If the system works when *any* component works → **parallel** (use $1 - \prod(1-R_i)$). (Lose 3 marks.)
> - Always report final answers to **4 decimal places** unless specified otherwise. Avoid premature rounding in intermediate steps.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Derive Bayes' Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. State all assumptions.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) In a reliability test, three independent components A, B, and C form a series system. Their reliabilities for a mission time of 1000 hours are $R_A = 0.92$, $R_B = 0.95$, and $R_C = 0.88$. Compute: (i) the system reliability, (ii) the system unreliability, and (iii) the probability that exactly one component fails while the other two survive.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (7 Marks)

**Assumption (1 mark):** Both events $A$ and $B$ have non-zero probability, i.e., $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$, so that conditioning is well-defined.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** By the definition of conditional probability, the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$ can be written in two symmetric ways:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Equating (i) and (ii) since both equal $P(A \cap B)$:

$$P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Dividing both sides by $P(A)$ (valid because $P(A) > 0$):

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Expressing $P(A)$ using the Law of Total Probability over a partition $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$:

$$\boxed{P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)}}$$

### Part (b) — Series System Reliability (7 Marks)

**(i) System Reliability (2 marks):**

For a series system with independent components, all components must survive:

$$R_s = R_A \cdot R_B \cdot R_C$$

$$R_s = (0.92)(0.95)(0.88)$$

**Sequential multiplication:**

$$(0.92)(0.95) = 0.8740$$

$$(0.8740)(0.88) = 0.76912$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 0.7691 \text{ (i.e., } 76.91\%\text{)}}$$

**(ii) System Unreliability (2 marks):**

By the complement rule:

$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.7691$$

$$\boxed{Q_s = 0.2309 \text{ (i.e., } 23.09\%\text{)}}$$

**(iii) Probability that exactly one component fails (3 marks):**

"Exactly one fails" means one fails and two survive. There are $\binom{3}{1} = 3$ ways to choose which component fails.

Let $F_i$ denote the failure of component $i$, with $P(F_i) = 1 - R_i$:

- $P(F_A) = 1 - 0.92 = 0.08$
- $P(F_B) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$
- $P(F_C) = 1 - 0.88 = 0.12$

By independence, the probability that *exactly* A fails (and B, C survive):

$$P_A = (0.08)(0.95)(0.88) = 0.066880$$

Probability that *exactly* B fails (and A, C survive):

$$P_B = (0.92)(0.05)(0.88) = 0.040480$$

Probability that *exactly* C fails (and A, B survive):

$$P_C = (0.92)(0.95)(0.12) = 0.104880$$

Total probability (sum of mutually exclusive cases):

$$P(\text{exactly one fails}) = P_A + P_B + P_C$$

$$P(\text{exactly one fails}) = 0.066880 + 0.040480 + 0.104880$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{exactly one fails}) = 0.2122 \text{ (i.e., } 21.22\%\text{)}}$$

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a) State and prove the Law of Total Probability. Explain its significance with an example from reliability engineering.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) An industrial control system uses two sensors S₁ and S₂ to monitor the same parameter. The probability that sensor S₁ detects a fault is 0.90, the probability that S₂ detects a fault is 0.85, and the probability that both detect a fault is 0.80. Find: (i) the probability that at least one sensor detects the fault, and (ii) the probability that S₁ detects the fault given that S₂ has detected it.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Law of Total Probability (7 Marks)

**Statement (2 marks):** Let $\{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n\}$ be a finite or countably infinite collection of mutually exclusive and exhaustive events (i.e., $B_i \cap B_j = \emptyset$ for $i \ne j$ and $\bigcup_i B_i = S$). Then for any event $A$ in the same sample space:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$

**Proof (3 marks):** Since the events $B_i$ form a partition of $S$, the event $A$ can be decomposed as a union of mutually exclusive sub-events:

$$A = A \cap S = A \cap \left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_i\right) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (A \cap B_i)$$

Since the $B_i$ are mutually exclusive, the events $A \cap B_i$ are also mutually exclusive. By the **third Kolmogorov axiom** of countable additivity:

$$P(A) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (A \cap B_i)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i)$$

By the definition of conditional probability, $P(A \cap B_i) = P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$, hence:

$$\boxed{P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}$$

**Significance in Reliability Engineering (2 marks):** The Law of Total Probability is used to compute the **aggregate failure probability** of a system when failures arise from multiple independent causes or operating environments. For example, if a pump fails due to bearing seizure (with probability 0.4), seal leakage (with probability 0.3), or impeller erosion (with probability 0.2), or other causes (with probability 0.1), the total failure probability is the sum of failure probabilities weighted by cause probabilities.

### Part (b) — Two-Sensor Fault Detection (7 Marks)

**Given:** $P(S_1) = 0.90$, $P(S_2) = 0.85$, $P(S_1 \cap S_2) = 0.80$.

**(i) Probability that at least one sensor detects the fault (3 marks):**

Use the general addition rule:

$$P(S_1 \cup S_2) = P(S_1) + P(S_2) - P(S_1 \cap S_2)$$

**Substituting values:**

$$P(S_1 \cup S_2) = 0.90 + 0.85 - 0.80 = 1.75 - 0.80$$

$$\boxed{P(S_1 \cup S_2) = 0.95}$$

> **Interpretation:** There is a **95% chance** that the fault is detected by at least one of the two sensors — a useful redundancy figure for designing alarm systems.

**(ii) Conditional probability $P(S_1 \mid S_2)$ (4 marks):**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(S_1 \mid S_2) = \frac{P(S_1 \cap S_2)}{P(S_2)}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$P(S_1 \mid S_2) = \frac{0.80}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{P(S_1 \mid S_2) = 0.9412 \text{ (i.e., } 94.12\%\text{)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Given that S₂ has detected the fault, the probability that S₁ has *also* detected it is about **94.12%**. This indicates strong *positive dependence* between the two sensors (if they were independent, $P(S_1 \mid S_2)$ would equal $P(S_1) = 0.90$; the higher value of 0.9412 suggests a common-cause coupling, which is critical information for designing **diverse-redundancy** systems in safety instrumented systems per IEC 61508).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Three definitions of probability:** Classical (equally likely outcomes), Empirical (relative frequency as $n \to \infty$), Axiomatic (Kolmogorov's three axioms). Know which definition applies to a given problem.

- **Kolmogorov's three axioms:** (i) $P(A) \ge 0$; (ii) $P(S) = 1$; (iii) countable additivity for mutually exclusive events. These are **not** theorems — they are the *axiomatic foundation* of probability.

- **Complement rule:** $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$. Foundation of reliability–unreliability duality: $R(t) + F(t) = 1$.

- **Addition rule (general):** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. For mutually exclusive events, the last term vanishes.

- **Conditional probability:** $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ provided $P(B) > 0$. The conditioning event must have strictly positive probability.

- **Multiplication rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$. For independent events: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

- **Independence vs. Mutual Exclusivity:** Two mutually exclusive events (with $P > 0$) are **always dependent**, never independent. This is a favourite KTU conceptual trap.

- **Law of Total Probability:** $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$, where $\{B_i\}$ is a partition of $S$. Use it to compute the marginal probability of an event with multiple causes.

- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(B_i \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)}{\sum_{j} P(A \mid B_j) P(B_j)}$. This is the **inferential engine** of reliability — it updates a prior probability upon receipt of new evidence.

- **Series System Reliability (independent components):** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$. All components must function. System reliability is always **less than or equal to** the weakest individual reliability.

- **Parallel System Reliability (independent components):** $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$. At least one component must function. System reliability is always **greater than or equal to** the strongest individual reliability.

- **Counting rule (combinations):** $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$. Used to count the number of ways to choose $k$ items from $n$.

- **De Morgan's Laws:** $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ and $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$. Useful for rewriting "at least one" / "none" probability statements.

- **Numerical hygiene:** Carry at least 4 decimal places in intermediate steps; do not round prematurely. State all assumptions (especially independence) explicitly before using any multiplication rule.

- **Reliability interpretation:** Every probability value $P \in [0, 1]$ in this module can be reinterpreted as a *long-run frequency* in a large population — a concept that bridges abstract probability and physical reliability data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addition of Probabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT611 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addition of Probabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Addition Rule of Probability (Theorem of Total Probability / Additive Law):**
> The Addition Rule of Probability is a fundamental theorem in probability theory that quantifies the probability of the **union (occurrence) of two or more events**. It states that the probability that at least one of several events occurs equals the sum of their individual probabilities, corrected for the probability of their simultaneous occurrence (overlap).

Mathematically, for any two events $A$ and $B$ defined on a sample space $S$:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

For **n events**, the general inclusion-exclusion principle applies:

$$P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right) = \sum_{i} P(A_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(A_i \cap A_j) + \sum_{i<j<k} P(A_i \cap A_j \cap A_k) - \cdots + (-1)^{n+1} P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n)$$

## 1.2 The Two Principal Cases (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 1 — Mutually Exclusive Events (Disjoint Sets):**
> Events $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive if they **cannot occur simultaneously**, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$ (the empty set). Therefore $P(A \cap B) = 0$.

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 2 — Non-Mutually Exclusive Events (Overlapping Sets):**
> Events $A$ and $B$ can occur together; hence $P(A \cap B) \neq 0$. The overlap is **subtracted once** to avoid double counting.

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Piggy Bank" Intuition

Imagine you have a piggy bank (your sample space, $S$) containing coins of two colours: **Red (A)** and **Blue (B)**.

- **Mutually Exclusive Case:** A coin is either red *or* blue, never both. To find the probability of drawing a red **or** a blue coin, you simply **add** the count of red coins to the count of blue coins. No overlap exists.
- **Non-Mutually Exclusive Case:** Some coins are **purple (A ∩ B)** — they are BOTH red and blue tinted. If you naively added the red coins and blue coins, you would **count the purple coins twice** (once as red, once as blue). So you must **subtract** the purple count once. This is precisely why the correction term $-P(A \cap B)$ exists.

> [!TIP]
> **Reliability Engineering Connection:** The Addition Rule is the *bedrock* of system unreliability calculations. When components fail **independently**, the probability of system failure (a union of individual failure events) is computed via this exact rule. For example, the probability that a redundant parallel system has *at least one* failed component is an addition-rule problem.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Bold

- **Sample Space ($S$):** The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment, with $P(S) = \mathbf{1}$ (i.e., **100% certainty**).
- **Certain Event:** $P(A) = \mathbf{1}$.
- **Impossible Event:** $P(\emptyset) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Empty Set ($\emptyset$):** Contains no elements; equivalent to "no overlap" in mutually exclusive events.
- **Boundary Condition:** $\mathbf{0 \leq P(A) \leq 1}$ for every event $A$.
- **Complement Rule:** $P(A^c) = \mathbf{1 - P(A)}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 'Why' Behind the Addition Rule — Set-Theoretic Foundation

The addition rule is a direct consequence of **counting disjoint regions** in a Venn diagram.

1. **Step 1 — Identify the universe:** The sample space $S$ contains all elementary outcomes.
2. **Step 2 — Define the regions:** Event $A$ occupies one region; event $B$ occupies another; their intersection $A \cap B$ is the common overlap region.
3. **Step 3 — Count union without correction:** Adding $P(A) + P(B)$ counts the overlap region **twice**.
4. **Step 4 — Apply correction:** Subtracting $P(A \cap B)$ restores the overlap to a **single count**, yielding $P(A \cup B)$.
5. **Step 5 — Validate boundary:** Since $P(A \cup B) \leq 1$, the formula must obey $P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \leq 1$, which is automatically satisfied for valid probability measures.

## 2.2 Logical Implications & Key Properties

- **Monotonicity:** If $A \subseteq B$, then $P(A) \leq P(B)$.
- **Sub-additivity (Boole's Inequality):** For any countable collection of events,
$$P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$
This is the **upper-bound form** of the addition rule — used extensively in reliability bounding (e.g., the **upper bound on system failure probability**).
- **Bonferroni Inequality (Lower Bound):**
$$P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right) \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(A_i \cap A_j)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Scenario | Formula | Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Two mutually exclusive events | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 2 | Two general events | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Always valid |
| 3 | Three mutually exclusive events | $P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C)$ | $A \cap B = B \cap C = A \cap C = \emptyset$ |
| 4 | Three general events | $P(A \cup B \cup C) = \sum P(A_i) - \sum P(A_i \cap A_j) + P(A \cap B \cap C)$ | Inclusion–Exclusion |
| 5 | Complement (At least one) | $P(A_1 \cup \cdots \cup A_n) = 1 - P(A_1^c \cap \cdots \cap A_n^c)$ | Useful shortcut |
| 6 | Boole's Upper Bound | $P(\bigcup A_i) \leq \sum P(A_i)$ | Always true |
| 7 | De Morgan's Law | $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ | Set identity |
| 8 | Probability of Empty Set | $P(\emptyset) = 0$ | Definition |
| 9 | Probability of Sample Space | $P(S) = 1$ | Axiom |
| 10 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | Derives from axiom |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Warning:** KTU examiners **strictly penalise** missing the intersection correction term. If events are *not* mutually exclusive, writing $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ without justification will lead to **mark deduction** of at least 2 out of 14 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **System Reliability (Series Systems):** A series system fails if **any** component fails. The system failure event is a union of component failure events. The unreliability $F_S(t) = P(\bigcup_i F_i)$ is computed via the addition rule.
- **Redundancy Allocation:** For a parallel system, the **success event** is "all components succeed," but the **failure event** is "at least one component fails" — a union. The Addition Rule gives the probability of failure.
- **Quality Control & Six-Sigma:** Probability that a batch contains **at least one defective item** is a union-probability calculation.
- **Risk Analysis:** Probability of **at least one hazard occurring** in a manufacturing plant.
- **Network Reliability:** Probability that **at least one path** in a communication network is operational.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Addition Rule from Axioms (Kolmogorov Framework)

### 3.1.1 Preliminary Set Identities

We begin with two fundamental set identities:

$$A \cup B = A \cup (B \cap A^c)$$

$$B = (B \cap A) \cup (B \cap A^c)$$

Both identities hold because $A \cup B$ can be partitioned into disjoint pieces.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Decompose $A \cup B$ into two **mutually exclusive** subsets.

$$A \cup B = A \cup (B - A)$$

where $B - A = B \cap A^c$ is the portion of $B$ that does not overlap with $A$.

**Step 2:** Since $A$ and $(B \cap A^c)$ are disjoint, apply the **finite additivity axiom**:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B \cap A^c)$$

**Step 3:** Decompose $B$ similarly.

$$B = (A \cap B) \cup (B \cap A^c)$$

Since $(A \cap B)$ and $(B \cap A^c)$ are disjoint:

$$P(B) = P(A \cap B) + P(B \cap A^c)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $P(B \cap A^c)$.

$$P(B \cap A^c) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into Step 2.

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.1.3 Extension to Three Events

Applying the inclusion–exclusion principle iteratively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \cup B \cup C) &= P\big((A \cup B) \cup C\big) \\
&= P(A \cup B) + P(C) - P\big((A \cup B) \cap C\big) \\
&= \big[P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\big] + P(C) - P\big((A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)\big) \\
&= P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) + P(A \cap B \cap C)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When expanding the three-event formula, always present the final line as $\sum P(A_i) - \sum P(A_i \cap A_j) + P(A \cap B \cap C)$. KTU examiners award full credit only when the **signs alternate** correctly.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — KTU Pattern

### Problem: A system has 3 independent failure modes with the following probabilities during a mission time $t$:

- $P(F_1) = 0.05$ (mechanical failure)
- $P(F_2) = 0.03$ (electrical failure)
- $P(F_3) = 0.02$ (thermal failure)

The system fails if **at least one** failure mode occurs. Joint probabilities:
- $P(F_1 \cap F_2) = 0.015$
- $P(F_1 \cap F_3) = 0.010$
- $P(F_2 \cap F_3) = 0.006$
- $P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3) = 0.002$

**Find the system failure probability and the system reliability.**

### Solution (Step-by-Step KTU Valuation Style):

**Step 1 — Identify the operation required.**
The system failure event is $F_S = F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3$ (at least one occurs).

**Step 2 — Apply the Inclusion–Exclusion Principle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_S) &= P(F_1) + P(F_2) + P(F_3) \\
&\quad - P(F_1 \cap F_2) - P(F_1 \cap F_3) - P(F_2 \cap F_3) \\
&\quad + P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_S) &= 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.02 \\
&\quad - 0.015 - 0.010 - 0.006 \\
&\quad + 0.002
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic carefully.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of singles} &= 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.02 = 0.10 \\
\text{Sum of pairs} &= 0.015 + 0.010 + 0.006 = 0.031 \\
\text{Triple intersection} &= 0.002
\end{aligned}
$$

$$P(F_S) = 0.10 - 0.031 + 0.002 = 0.071$$

**Step 5 — Compute System Reliability.**
System reliability is the complement of unreliability:

$$R_S(t) = 1 - P(F_S) = 1 - 0.071 = 0.929$$

**Step 6 — State final answers with units.**
- System Unreliability: $F_S(t) = 0.071$ (dimensionless probability, or **7.1%**)
- System Reliability: $R_S(t) = 0.929$ (dimensionless probability, or **92.9%**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check:** Since all events are non-mutually exclusive, Boole's inequality gives an upper bound $P(F_S) \leq 0.10$. Our answer $P(F_S) = 0.071$ satisfies this bound — the calculation is consistent. $\checkmark$

### 3.3 Alternative Solution via the Complement Method

For verification, we can use De Morgan's law:

$$P(F_S) = 1 - P(F_1^c \cap F_2^c \cap F_3^c)$$

where $F_i^c$ means failure mode $i$ does **not** occur. With independence assumption among the complements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_1^c \cap F_2^c \cap F_3^c) &= P(F_1^c) \cdot P(F_2^c) \cdot P(F_3^c) \\
&= (1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.02) \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.98 \\
&= 0.90313
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$P(F_S) = 1 - 0.90313 = 0.09687 \approx 0.097$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why the discrepancy?** The two methods give **different answers** ($0.071$ vs $0.097$) because the events are **not independent** in the first method (joint probabilities are given explicitly). KTU examiners frequently test whether students recognise that the *Independence Assumption* is crucial for the complement-shortcut method. Use the **Inclusion–Exclusion method** whenever joint probabilities are explicitly provided.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Venn Diagram of the Addition Rule (Two Events)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Sample Space S - Universe] --> A[Event A - red region]
    S --> B[Event B - blue region]
    A --> AnB[Intersection A AND B - purple overlap]
    B --> AnB
    AnB --> Label1["P(A ∩ B) counted TWICE if we add P(A) + P(B)"]
    Label1 --> Label2["Subtract P(A ∩ B) ONCE to correct → P(A ∪ B)"]
    style S fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    style AnB fill:#d4a5d4,stroke:#606,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — System Failure Probability Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["Input: Component Failure Probabilities P(Fi)"] --> Check{Step 1: Are events mutually exclusive?}
    Check -- YES --> MutExc["Apply Simple Addition: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B)"]
    Check -- NO --> NonMutExc["Step 2: Find intersection P(A∩B)"]
    NonMutExc --> Step3["Step 3: Apply General Rule: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A∩B)"]
    MutExc --> Validate["Step 4: Validate via Boole Bound"]
    Step3 --> Validate
    Validate --> Output["Output: P(A∪B) - System Failure Probability"]
    Output --> Reliability["System Reliability R = 1 - P(A∪B)"]
    style Input fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
    style Check fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px
    style Output fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:1px
    style Reliability fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Three-Event Inclusion-Exclusion Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S2[Sample Space S] --> R1[Region A only]
    S2 --> R2[Region B only]
    S2 --> R3[Region C only]
    S2 --> R4[Region A and B only - pair intersection]
    S2 --> R5[Region A and C only - pair intersection]
    S2 --> R6[Region B and C only - pair intersection]
    S2 --> R7[Region A and B and C - triple intersection]
    R1 --> Sum[Sum all individual P Ai]
    R2 --> Sum
    R3 --> Sum
    R4 --> Sub[Subtract all pair intersections]
    R5 --> Sub
    R6 --> Sub
    R7 --> Add[Add triple intersection back]
    Sum --> Final[P of A union B union C]
    Sub --> Final
    Add --> Final
    style S2 fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Final fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Formula

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Given: Two or more events] --> Q1{Do they overlap?}
    Q1 -- No, Disjoint --> Formula1["P A U B = P A + P B"]
    Q1 -- Yes, Overlapping --> Q2{Intersection given?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Formula2["P A U B = P A + P B - P A ∩ B"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Independent?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Formula3["P A U B = 1 - P Ac x P Bc"]
    Q3 -- No --> Formula4["Need additional info - cannot solve directly"]
    style Q fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    style Formula1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060
    style Formula2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060
    style Formula3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#069
    style Formula4 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#c00
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and prove the addition rule of probability for two events.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (1 Mark):** For any two events $A$ and $B$ in a sample space $S$, the probability of their union is:
> $$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Proof (2 Marks):**

We know that $A \cup B = A \cup (B \cap A^c)$ where $A$ and $(B \cap A^c)$ are mutually exclusive.

By the additivity axiom:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B \cap A^c)$$

Also, $B = (A \cap B) \cup (B \cap A^c)$, and these are mutually exclusive, so:

$$P(B) = P(A \cap B) + P(B \cap A^c) \implies P(B \cap A^c) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Special Case (1 Bonus Mark if mentioned):** When $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, $P(A \cap B) = 0$, giving $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between mutually exclusive and mutually non-exclusive events with a suitable example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutually Exclusive Events (1.5 Marks):** Two events $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive if they **cannot occur simultaneously**, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$, hence $P(A \cap B) = 0$. The occurrence of one rules out the other.
>
> **Example:** When a die is rolled, getting an even number ($A = \{2,4,6\}$) and getting an odd number ($B = \{1,3,5\}$) are mutually exclusive since $A \cap B = \emptyset$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutually Non-Exclusive Events (1.5 Marks):** Two events $A$ and $B$ are mutually non-exclusive if they **can occur simultaneously**, i.e., $A \cap B \neq \emptyset$, hence $P(A \cap B) > 0$.
>
> **Example:** Drawing a King ($A$) and drawing a Heart ($B$) from a deck of 52 cards. The King of Hearts satisfies both, so $A \cap B \neq \emptyset$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the probability of the union of three events $A$, $B$, and $C$ using the inclusion–exclusion principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a manufacturing plant, three independent machines have failure probabilities $P(F_1) = 0.10$, $P(F_2) = 0.15$, and $P(F_3) = 0.20$ during a shift. Calculate the probability that **at least one** machine fails using:
   (i) The inclusion–exclusion formula **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) The complement method with independence assumption **(4 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Three-Event Inclusion-Exclusion (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Apply the two-event formula to $(A \cup B)$ and $C$:

$$P(A \cup B \cup C) = P\big((A \cup B) \cup C\big) = P(A \cup B) + P(C) - P\big((A \cup B) \cap C\big)$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substitute the two-event formula for $P(A \cup B)$ and distribute the intersection:

$$(A \cup B) \cap C = (A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)$$

Therefore:
$$P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) + P(C) - P\big((A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)\big)$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the two-event formula again to $(A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)$:

$$P\big((A \cap C) \cup (B \cap C)\big) = P(A \cap C) + P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Combine all terms:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
P(A \cup B \cup C) =\;& P(A) + P(B) + P(C) \\
&- P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) \\
&+ P(A \cap B \cap C)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Inclusion–Exclusion Method (3 Marks):**

Since events are independent, all intersections factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_1 \cap F_2) &= 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.015 \\
P(F_1 \cap F_3) &= 0.10 \times 0.20 = 0.020 \\
P(F_2 \cap F_3) &= 0.15 \times 0.20 = 0.030 \\
P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3) &= 0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 = 0.003
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying inclusion-exclusion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3) &= 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.20 - 0.015 - 0.020 - 0.030 + 0.003 \\
&= 0.45 - 0.065 + 0.003 \\
&= 0.388
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Complement Method (4 Marks):**

By De Morgan's law:

$$P(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3) = 1 - P(F_1^c \cap F_2^c \cap F_3^c)$$

Under independence, the complements are also independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(F_1^c \cap F_2^c \cap F_3^c) &= (1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.15)(1 - 0.20) \\
&= 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.612
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$P(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3) = 1 - 0.612 = 0.388$$

Both methods yield **$P = 0.388$** or **38.8%** — confirming consistency. ✓

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and explain Boole's inequality. How does it serve as an upper bound for the addition rule? Use a Venn diagram interpretation in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A communication system has 4 redundant channels. The probability that any individual channel fails during a 24-hour period is 0.08. Assuming channels fail independently:
   (i) Compute the probability that **at least one** channel fails using the addition rule. **(4 Marks)**
   (ii) If the channels were **perfectly correlated** (all fail or all succeed together), recalculate the probability. **(3 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Boole's Inequality (7 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** For any countable sequence of events $A_1, A_2, \ldots$:

$$P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

**Explanation (3 Marks):** Boole's inequality states that the probability of the union of events is **at most** the sum of their individual probabilities. The inequality becomes an **equality** if and only if the events are mutually exclusive. This provides a quick **upper-bound estimate** for system failure probability without needing to compute all intersection terms.

**Venn Diagram Interpretation (2 Marks):** In a Venn diagram, $P(A) + P(B)$ counts the overlap $P(A \cap B)$ twice, hence $P(A) + P(B) \geq P(A \cup B)$. The "extra" mass beyond $P(A \cup B)$ equals exactly $P(A \cap B)$, the doubly-counted overlap region. Therefore:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \leq P(A) + P(B)$$

This is Boole's inequality for the two-event case.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Independent Channels — Addition Rule via Complement (4 Marks):**

By De Morgan's law:

$$P(\text{at least one fails}) = 1 - P(\text{none fails}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{4} (1 - 0.08)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{at least one fails}) &= 1 - (0.92)^4 \\
&= 1 - 0.92 \times 0.92 \times 0.92 \times 0.92 \\
&= 1 - 0.71639 \\
&= 0.28361 \approx 0.2836
\end{aligned}
$$

So the probability is approximately **28.36%**.

**(ii) Perfectly Correlated Channels (3 Marks):**

If all channels are perfectly correlated, they all fail together with probability 0.08 or all succeed together with probability 0.92. So:

- $P(\text{all 4 fail}) = 0.08$
- $P(\text{all 4 succeed}) = 0.92$

"At least one fails" means "all fail" in this correlated scenario:

$$P(\text{at least one fails}) = P(\text{all 4 fail}) = 0.08$$

So the probability is **8%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The probability drops from **28.36%** (independent) to **8%** (perfectly correlated). This demonstrates a critical reliability principle: **redundancy provides the greatest benefit when components fail independently**, not when they fail together.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS IN ADDITION-OF-PROBABILITIES QUESTIONS**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the intersection correction ($-P(A \cap B)$):** Costs **2 to 4 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always check if events are mutually exclusive.
> 2. **Sign errors in three-event inclusion-exclusion:** Students often write $-P(A \cap B \cap C)$ instead of $+P(A \cap B \cap C)$. The sign **alternates** — memorise the pattern: $+, -, +$.
> 3. **Misapplying independence:** When using the complement shortcut $1 - \prod P(A_i^c)$, the **independence assumption must be explicitly stated**. Otherwise, full credit is not awarded.
> 4. **Confusing union and intersection:** "$A$ and $B$" means $A \cap B$ (multiplication rule), while "$A$ or $B$" means $A \cup B$ (addition rule). KTU examiners **routinely test** this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating boundary condition:** Failing to verify $0 \leq P(A \cup B) \leq 1$ at the end of a numerical answer is a **1-mark penalty** in valuation.
> 6. **Misnaming the union notation:** Writing $P(AB)$ instead of $P(A \cup B)$ for the "at least one" event is **non-standard** and may lose marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Addition of Probabilities**

- ✅ The **Addition Rule** gives $P(A \cup B)$, the probability that **at least one** of the events occurs.
- ✅ For **mutually exclusive** events: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.
- ✅ For **general** events: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.
- ✅ The **intersection term** $P(A \cap B)$ is subtracted to **avoid double counting** the overlap region.
- ✅ The **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle** generalises to $n$ events with alternating signs.
- ✅ For three events: $P(A \cup B \cup C) = \sum P(A_i) - \sum P(A_i \cap A_j) + P(A \cap B \cap C)$.
- ✅ **Boole's Inequality** provides the upper bound: $P(\bigcup A_i) \leq \sum P(A_i)$.
- ✅ **Complement shortcut:** $P(\bigcup A_i) = 1 - P(\bigcap A_i^c)$, valid only under **independence**.
- ✅ **De Morgan's Law:** $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ — essential for complement-method problems.
- ✅ In **series-system reliability**, system failure is a **union** of component failures — directly computed via the addition rule.
- ✅ **Boundary check:** Always verify $0 \leq P(A \cup B) \leq 1$ at the end of numerical problems.
- ✅ **Examiner pattern:** KTU ESE Part B questions on this topic usually feature 2 to 3 sub-parts combining derivation + numerical application + special-case analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complementary Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT611 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Complementary Events

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
