> **Examination Favourite:** Constant hazard models dominate the analysis of **electronic components, complex systems with many independent sub-systems**, and components operating within their **useful life** (Region II of the classic "bathtub curve"). Practically every reliability textbook begins with this model.

The constant hazard assumption leads to extraordinarily clean, mathematically tractable results, which is why it remains the **default assumption** in countless engineering standards (MIL-HDBK-217, Telcordia SR-332, IEC 61709, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundations

The hazard function is rigorously defined from the conditional probability of failure:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where:
- $f(t)$ = probability density function (pdf) of failure time
- $R(t) = P(T > t)$ = reliability (survival) function
- $T$ = random variable representing time-to-failure

When $h(t) = \lambda$ is **constant**, the differential equation governing $R(t)$ becomes trivial to solve, leading to the **exponential distribution**.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Constant Hazard Concept

Let us step through the logic chain that makes constant hazard special:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Hazard:** The hazard at time $t$ quantifies the *conditional* failure intensity — only considering components that have *already survived* up to $t$.
2. **Step 2 — Apply the Constant Assumption:** We postulate that $h(t)$ does not depend on $t$, i.e., the failure intensity is the same for a brand-new component and for one that has run for 10 years.
3. **Step 3 — Solve the Reliability ODE:** Using $h(t) = - \frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t)$, we obtain a first-order linear ODE with constant coefficient.
4. **Step 4 — Identify the Distribution:** The resulting solution is uniquely the **exponential distribution**.
5. **Step 5 — Exploit the Memoryless Property:** Constant hazard implies $P(T > t+s \mid T > t) = P(T > s)$, meaning past survival history is irrelevant.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET** — Memorize all entries below; they form ~70% of marks in constant-hazard problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \lambda$ | failures per unit time |
| 2 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Cumulative Distribution Function | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 4 | Probability Density Function | $f(t) = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$ |
| 5 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours, cycles, km, etc. |
| 6 | Median Life ($t_{0.5}$) | $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Time for 50% failures |
| 7 | Mode of Distribution | $t_{\text{mode}} = 0$ | Failures most likely at $t = 0^+$ |
| 8 | Variance of Life | $\text{Var}(T) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ | — |
| 9 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equals the MTTF |
| 10 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid t_{0}) = e^{-\lambda (t - t_{0})}$ | For $t > t_{0}$ |
| 11 | Mission Reliability | $R(t_{m}) = e^{-\lambda t_{m}}$ | For mission duration $t_{m}$ |
| 12 | Memoryless Property | $R(t+s \mid T>t) = R(s) = e^{-\lambda s}$ | Independent of past |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The constant hazard model is the **workhorse** of reliability engineering practice:

- **Electronic components** (resistors, capacitors, ICs in useful life) — random failure dominates.
- **Aerospace structures** subject to random fatigue cycles from gust loading.
- **Software reliability** modeling (in some simplified treatments) — bug discovery rate approximates constant.
- **Series systems** of many independent components: by the exponential property, the *system hazard* equals the *sum of component hazards* — making reliability aggregation remarkably easy.
- **Maintainable systems** subject to **random shocks** between preventive overhauls (the overhaul "resets" the wear-in/wear-out behavior to the useful-life regime).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If a KTU problem states "the failure rate is constant at $\lambda = X$ failures/hour" without specifying a time-varying model, the student should *immediately* recognize this as a constant hazard (exponential) problem and apply the formulas above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function from Constant Hazard

**Starting Point:** Definition of hazard function in terms of reliability:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the constant hazard assumption:**

$$-\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) = \lambda$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the derivative:**

$$\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) = -\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides with respect to $t$:**

$$\int \frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) \, dt = -\int \lambda \, dt$$

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t + C$$

**Step 4 — Apply the initial condition** $R(0) = 1$ (component is 100% reliable at $t = 0$):

$$\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0 = -\lambda(0) + C \implies C = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R(t)$ by exponentiating both sides:**

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Probability Density Function

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship between pdf and reliability:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:**

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = -\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the negative sign:**

$$\boxed{f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0}$$

This is the **exponential distribution** with rate parameter $\lambda$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of expected value for a non-negative random variable:**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral using integration by parts:**

Let $u = t \implies du = dt$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt \implies v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term:**

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \left(-t e^{-\lambda t}\right) = 0 \quad \text{(since exponential decay dominates linear growth)}$$

$$\left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the remaining integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Memoryless Property

**Step 1 — Recall the conditional probability definition:**

$$R(t+s \mid T > t) = \frac{R(t+s)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the constant-hazard reliability function:**

$$R(t+s \mid T > t) = \frac{e^{-\lambda(t+s)}}{e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponential expression:**

$$R(t+s \mid T > t) = e^{-\lambda t - \lambda s + \lambda t} = e^{-\lambda s}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the right-hand side:**

$$e^{-\lambda s} = R(s)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R(t+s \mid T > t) = R(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The probability that a component survives an *additional* $s$ hours, given it has already survived $t$ hours, is identical to the probability that a *brand-new* component survives $s$ hours. **The component "forgets" its past.** This is the defining characteristic of constant hazard.

### 3.5 Derivation of the Median Life (50% Failure Time)

**Step 1 — Set $R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$:**

$$e^{-\lambda t_{0.5}} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$-\lambda t_{0.5} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_{0.5}$:**

$$\boxed{t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.6931}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Quantity Verification Table

The following table summarizes the verification of consistency across all derived formulas:

| Quantity | Expression | Numerical Check ($\lambda = 0.001$/hr) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R(0)$ | $e^{0} = 1$ | 1 | 100% reliable at start |
| $R(1000)$ | $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ | 0.3679 | ~36.8% survive 1000 hr |
| $F(1000)$ | $1 - e^{-1}$ | 0.6321 | ~63.2% fail by 1000 hr |
| MTTF | $1/0.001$ | 1000 hr | Mean life |
| Median | $\ln 2 / 0.001$ | 693.1 hr | 50% fail point |
| $f(0)$ | $\lambda$ | 0.001/hr | Peak failure density |
| $\sigma$ | $1/\lambda$ | 1000 hr | Std. dev. equals mean |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check insight:** Notice that $\sigma = \text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. This peculiarity (mean equals standard deviation) is **unique** to the exponential distribution and is a frequent KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Constant Hazard System Model

The following diagram shows the *functional architecture* of how a constant-hazard model maps failure-time observations into the reliability quantities used in engineering analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Operating System with Constant Hazard h(t) equals lambda"] --> nodeB["Differential Equation: dR/dt equals minus lambda R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Solution: R of t equals exp of minus lambda t"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Survival Probability R of t"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Failure Probability F of t equals 1 minus R of t"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Failure Density f of t equals lambda times exp of minus lambda t"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Mission Reliability R of tm"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Conditional Reliability R of t given t0"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Mean Time To Failure MTTF equals 1 over lambda"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Median Life equals ln 2 over lambda"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["Variance equals 1 over lambda squared"]
    nodeG --> nodeL["Engineering Decision: Maintain, Replace, or Accept Risk"]
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Memoryless Property of Constant Hazard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Component Survives t hours"] --> node2["Conditional Probability of Surviving Additional s hours"]
    node2 --> node3["R of t plus s given T greater than t equals exp of minus lambda s"]
    node3 --> node4["Equivalent to R of s for a brand new component"]
    node4 --> node5["Conclusion: Past survival does NOT influence future hazard"]
```

### 4.3 Reliability Function Decay Curve — Conceptual Schematic

The reliability function under constant hazard exhibits **exponential decay**. The following mermaid block diagram conveys this behavior structurally:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Initial State at t equals 0: R of 0 equals 1 Point 0 zero zero percent failed"] --> nodeB["At t equals MTTF equals 1 over lambda: R of t equals 1 over e equals 0 Point 3 6 7 9 about 36 Point 8 percent surviving"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["At t equals Median equals ln 2 over lambda: R of t equals 0 Point 5 0 percent surviving"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["As t approaches infinity: R of t approaches 0"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Asymptotic Behavior: All components eventually fail"]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block — Constant vs Non-Constant Hazard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Hazard Function h of t"] --> nodeB["Constant: h of t equals lambda"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Increasing: Wear Out e.g. Weibull with beta greater than 1"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Decreasing: Infant Mortality e.g. Weibull with beta less than 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Exponential Distribution, Memoryless, Useful Life Region"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Weibull or Log normal, Aging Dominant"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Burn in Period, Early Failures Dominant"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term **constant hazard rate** in reliability engineering. Mention the probability distribution to which it corresponds.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **constant hazard rate** $h(t) = \lambda$ is the instantaneous failure rate of a component that remains **independent of operating time** $t$. It implies that the probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval $dt$ is the same whether the component is brand-new or has run for years.

This constant hazard condition corresponds to the **exponential distribution**, characterized by:
- $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$
- $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "$h(t) = \lambda$" without stating that $\lambda$ is **independent of time**. This costs 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series I]**

State and briefly explain the **memoryless property** of the constant hazard model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The memoryless property states that for a constant-hazard system, the probability of surviving an additional time $s$, given survival up to time $t$, is the same as the probability that a brand-new component survives time $s$:

$$R(t+s \mid T > t) = R(s) = e^{-\lambda s}$$

This means the component "has no memory" of its past operating history. *(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> A complex electronic system has a constant hazard rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour. Calculate:
> (i) The reliability for a mission duration of 500 hours.
> (ii) The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).
> (iii) The probability that the system will fail within the first 200 hours.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> For the system in part (a), if the system has already survived 300 hours, find the probability that it will survive an additional 400 hours. Comment on the result using the memoryless property.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour, $t_m = 500$ hr.

**(i) Reliability for 500-hour mission** [2 marks]:

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 500} = e^{-0.25}$$

$$R(500) = 0.7788$$

**Answer:** $R(500) \approx 0.7788$ (about **77.88%** survive the 500-hour mission).

**(ii) Mean Time To Failure** [2 marks]:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** MTTF = **2000 hours**.

**(iii) Probability of failure within first 200 hours** [3 marks]:

$$F(200) = 1 - R(200) = 1 - e^{-0.0005 \times 200} = 1 - e^{-0.1}$$

$$F(200) = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952$$

**Answer:** $F(200) \approx 0.0952$ (about **9.52%** chance of failure in first 200 hours).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Required:** $P(T > 300 + 400 \mid T > 300) = R(400)$ by memoryless property.

**Step 1 — Apply the memoryless formula directly** [3 marks]:

$$R(300 + 400 \mid T > 300) = R(400) = e^{-0.0005 \times 400} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$R(400) = 0.8187$$

**Step 2 — Cross-verification via direct calculation** [2 marks]:

$$R(700 \mid T > 300) = \frac{R(700)}{R(300)} = \frac{e^{-0.35}}{e^{-0.15}} = e^{-0.20} = 0.8187 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Comment** [2 marks]:

The result $R(400) = 0.8187$ is **identical** to the reliability of a *brand-new* system for a 400-hour mission. This confirms the **memoryless property** — the past 300 hours of survival has no influence on the system's future hazard behavior.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]`

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ for a system subjected to a constant hazard rate $\lambda$, starting from the definition $h(t) = -d(\ln R)/dt$.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> A machine has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.02$ failures per day. Determine:
> (i) The probability that the machine will operate without failure for at least 30 days.
> (ii) The median life of the machine.
> (iii) The reliability for a 100-day mission if preventive maintenance resets the hazard rate at the start of the mission.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1** [1 mark]: Write the definition:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t)$$

**Step 2** [1 mark]: Substitute $h(t) = \lambda$:

$$-\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) = \lambda$$

**Step 3** [1 mark]: Rearrange:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t) = -\lambda$$

**Step 4** [1 mark]: Integrate:

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t + C$$

**Step 5** [1 mark]: Apply initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0 \implies C = 0$$

**Step 6** [1 mark]: Solve for $R(t)$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t \implies R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 7** [1 mark]: Final boxed statement with interpretation:

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

This is the reliability function corresponding to the **exponential distribution** — the only distribution exhibiting constant hazard.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.02$ failures/day.

**(i) Reliability for at least 30 days** [3 marks]:

$$R(30) = e^{-0.02 \times 30} = e^{-0.6} = 0.5488$$

**Answer:** Probability of surviving at least 30 days ≈ **0.5488 or 54.88%**.

**(ii) Median life** [2 marks]:

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{0.02} = 34.66 \text{ days}$$

**Answer:** Median life ≈ **34.66 days**.

**(iii) Reliability for 100-day mission after reset** [2 marks]:

After reset, $\lambda = 0.02$ /day applies from $t = 0$ to $t = 100$:

$$R(100) = e^{-0.02 \times 100} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$$

**Answer:** $R(100) \approx$ **0.1353 or 13.53%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write "failures/hour", "hours", etc. Lose 1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Misapplying the memoryless property** — In part (b) of Question A, students compute $R(700)$ instead of $R(400)$. The correct approach uses conditional probability.
> 3. **Sign errors in derivation** — In deriving $R(t)$, students drop the negative sign when separating variables. Carefully write $\frac{d(\ln R)}{dt} = -\lambda$.
> 4. **Forgetting boundary condition $R(0) = 1$** — Without this, the integration constant $C$ remains undetermined. KTU specifically awards 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF** — For non-repairable systems, the correct term is **MTTF**. KTU uses both terms in different questions; match the question's phrasing.
> 6. **Not commenting on memoryless property** — When asked to "comment," state explicitly that past survival has **no influence** on future hazard.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Constant Hazard: Introduction**

- ☐ **Core Definition:** $h(t) = \lambda$ = constant; corresponds to **exponential distribution**.
- ☐ **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Failure Probability (CDF):** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Density (pdf):** $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **MTTF:** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- ☐ **Median Life:** $\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$.
- ☐ **Variance:** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$.
- ☐ **Standard Deviation = MTTF:** $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (a unique property of exponential distribution).
- ☐ **Memoryless Property:** $R(t+s \mid T > t) = R(s)$ — past survival is irrelevant.
- ☐ **Boundary Condition:** $R(0) = 1$ (must apply during derivation).
- ☐ **Useful-Life Region:** Constant hazard models the flat middle portion of the bathtub curve.
- ☐ **Engineering Applications:** Electronic components, complex systems, random shock environments, software bugs (in simplified treatments).
- ☐ **Series System Hazard:** $\lambda_{\text{system}} = \sum \lambda_i$ (exponential property).
- ☐ **Assumption Validity:** Only when wear-out and infant-mortality effects are negligible.
- ☐ **Default KTU Assumption:** Whenever a problem says "constant failure rate" without further qualification, apply the exponential formulas directly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearly Increasing Hazard, Weibull Model, Density Function and Distribution Function, Reliability Analysis, Important Distributions and Their Choice, Standard Deviation and Variance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT611 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linearly Increasing Hazard, Weibull Model, Density Function and Distribution Function, Reliability Analysis, Important Distributions and Their Choice, Standard Deviation and Variance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) model** is a non-constant hazard-rate model in which the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ grows as a **linear function of operating time $t$**. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta t, \quad \alpha \geq 0,\ \beta \geq 0,\ t \geq 0$$

Here, **$\alpha$** is the *initial hazard rate* (intercept at $t=0$) and **$\beta$** is the *rate of increase of hazard* (slope). This model is widely used to describe systems undergoing gradual wear-out, such as rolling-element bearings, mechanical seals, and electrolytic capacitors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** LIH is the **simplest wear-out model**. The Exponential distribution is a special case when $\beta = 0$ and $\alpha = \lambda$ (constant hazard). When $\alpha = 0$, the model reduces to a **Rayleigh distribution**, which is a special case of the Weibull distribution.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine the maintenance schedule of a new car. When the car is freshly purchased, breakdowns are rare (low hazard at $t=0$). As mileage accumulates, the engine oil degrades, brake pads thin out, and gaskets harden. The probability of breakdown per kilometre keeps *growing* with the car's age — not exponentially, but roughly in a straight-line fashion. That monotonic, linear growth of "breakdown probability" with time is the physical meaning of **Linearly Increasing Hazard**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** All times in this module are expressed in standard SI units — hours (h), kilometres (km), or cycles — depending on the system under study.

---

## 1.2 Weibull Model — The Heart of Reliability Engineering

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Weibull distribution**, introduced by Swedish engineer **Waloddi Weibull in 1939**, is a flexible continuous probability distribution used to model time-to-failure data. It is described by three parameters:

- **Shape parameter $\beta > 0$** — controls the shape of the hazard rate (mortality behaviour).
- **Scale parameter $\eta > 0$** — stretches or compresses the distribution along the time axis.
- **Location parameter $\gamma$** — represents the failure-free operating period (often taken as 0 for simplicity).

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** is given by:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq \gamma$$

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** is:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

The **Reliability (Survival) Function** is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

The **Hazard Rate Function** is:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the Weibull distribution as a "master key" for reliability analysis. Just as a single key can open many types of locks, a single Weibull distribution — by adjusting $\beta$ — can mimic:

- **Infant mortality** ($\beta < 1$): Decreasing hazard. Like a newly-hatched chick, weakest at birth, stronger with time.
- **Random failures** ($\beta = 1$): Constant hazard. Equivalent to the Exponential distribution. Like a mature adult in their prime.
- **Wear-out failures** ($\beta > 1$): Increasing hazard. Like an ageing person whose health deteriorates with time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Weibull distribution is the **most exam-frequent** distribution in Reliability Engineering. Always check what value of $\beta$ is given in the problem before applying the Exponential, Rayleigh, or general Weibull formulas.

---

## 1.3 Density Function vs. Distribution Function

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | $f(t)$ | Instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$. Area under the curve over an interval gives probability of failure in that interval. |
| **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** | $F(t)$ | Total probability of failure by time $t$ (cumulative). |
| **Reliability Function (Survival Function)** | $R(t)$ | Probability that the system survives beyond time $t$. |

The fundamental relationship connecting all three is:

$$F(t) + R(t) = 1 \quad \text{(since the system either fails or survives by time } t\text{)}$$

And,

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

---

## 1.4 Standard Deviation and Variance in Reliability

### Formal Definition
**Variance ($\sigma^2$)** measures the *average squared deviation* of failure times from the mean, indicating the spread of failure data.

$$\sigma^2 = E[T^2] - [E[T]]^2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 f(t)\, dt - \mu^2$$

**Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** is the positive square root of variance and shares the same units as the random variable (time, in hours).

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$

For the **Weibull distribution** ($\gamma = 0$):

$$\mu = \eta\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\sigma^2 = \eta^2\left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left\{\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right\}^2\right]$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the **Gamma function**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Gamma function is a generalisation of the factorial: $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$ for positive integers, and $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(0.5) = \sqrt{\pi}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) — Theoretical Decomposition

The LIH model assumes:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta t$$

### Step 1 — Cumulative Hazard Function
The cumulative hazard $H(t)$ is the integral of hazard rate from 0 to $t$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = \int_{0}^{t} (\alpha + \beta u)\, du = \alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Reliability Function
By the fundamental reliability identity $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Cumulative Distribution Function

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Probability Density Function

$$f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t) = (\alpha + \beta t)\, e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}$$

### Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)
For LIH, the MTTF is computed by:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)} dt$$

This integral has the closed-form expression in terms of a special function:

$$\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}\, e^{\alpha^2 / (2\beta)} \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)$$

where $\text{erfc}(\cdot)$ is the **complementary error function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case — Rayleigh Distribution:** If $\alpha = 0$ in LIH, the model reduces to the **Rayleigh distribution**:
> $$\lambda(t) = \beta t,\quad R(t) = e^{-\beta t^2 / 2}$$
> This is a special case of the Weibull distribution with $\beta_{W} = 2$ and $\eta_{W} = \sqrt{2/\beta}$.

---

## 2.2 Weibull Model — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The Weibull hazard rate:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

reveals three behavioural regimes based on the value of $\beta$:

| Shape Parameter $\beta$ | Hazard Behaviour | Reliability Phase | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing | Infant mortality / burn-in | Electronic components with manufacturing defects |
| $\beta = 1$ | Constant ($\lambda = 1/\eta$) | Useful life / random failures | Memory chips, complex systems in service |
| $1 < \beta < 2$ | Slowly increasing | Early wear-out | Bearings, mechanical fatigue |
| $\beta > 2$ | Rapidly increasing | Severe wear-out | Old equipment near end of life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Weibull" is the King of Reliability:** Many classical distributions are special cases or approximations of the Weibull:
> - **Exponential:** $\beta = 1$
> - **Rayleigh:** $\beta = 2$, $\eta = \sqrt{2/\sigma^2}$
> - **Approximation to Normal:** $\beta \approx 3.25$ to $3.6$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 80% of the numerical questions asked in KTU exams for Module 2.**

| # | Quantity | LIH Model | Weibull Model (2-parameter, $\gamma = 0$) | Rayleigh ($\alpha = 0$ in LIH) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ | $\alpha + \beta t$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\beta t$ |
| 2 | Cumulative hazard $H(t)$ | $\alpha t + \dfrac{\beta t^2}{2}$ | $\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\beta t^2}{2}$ |
| 3 | Reliability $R(t)$ | $\exp\!\left(-\alpha t - \dfrac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)$ | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $e^{-\beta t^2/2}$ |
| 4 | CDF $F(t)$ | $1 - R(t)$ | $1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $1 - e^{-\beta t^2/2}$ |
| 5 | PDF $f(t)$ | $(\alpha + \beta t) e^{-\left(\alpha t + \beta t^2/2\right)}$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\beta t\, e^{-\beta t^2/2}$ |
| 6 | Mean / MTTF $\mu$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2\beta}}\, e^{\alpha^2/2\beta}\, \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)$ | $\eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2\beta}} = \eta\, \Gamma(1.5)$ |
| 7 | Variance $\sigma^2$ | (Computed numerically) | $\eta^2\left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\eta^2\left[\Gamma(2) - \Gamma^2(1.5)\right] = \eta^2\left(1 - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ |

### Useful Gamma Function Values (frequently needed)

| $x$ | $\Gamma(x)$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1 |
| 1.5 | $\sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$ |
| 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 2 |
| 4 | 6 |

---

## 2.4 Choice of Distribution — Engineering Decision Framework

Choosing the correct distribution is the most critical step in reliability analysis. The following decision matrix is invaluable:

| Phenomenon | Recommended Distribution | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Constant hazard (random / memoryless) | **Exponential** | Simple, memoryless property, MTTF = $1/\lambda$ |
| Components with infant mortality | **Weibull with $\beta < 1$** | Decreasing hazard captures early-life failures |
| Wear-out with gradual degradation | **Weibull with $\beta > 1$** (often 2–3) | Increasing hazard captures ageing |
| Mild wear-out, no initial failure | **LIH / Rayleigh** | Linear hazard approximates moderate wear |
| Fatigue, crack propagation | **Weibull with $\beta \approx 2$–$4$** | Matches cumulative damage models |
| Repair times | **Lognormal** | Skewed distribution, multiplicative degradation |
| Strength of materials, tolerances | **Normal** | Symmetric distribution, central limit theorem |
| Extreme events (max load, max stress) | **Gumbel (Extreme Value Type I)** | Asymptotic distribution of maxima |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common 7-mark question asks, *"For a given engineering scenario, justify the choice of a particular distribution."* Always cite **two reasons**: (1) physical mechanism of failure, and (2) the shape of the hazard rate implied by the scenario.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From LIH to Weibull via Hazard Integration

We start with the most general LIH hazard:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta t$$

### Step 1 — Integrate the hazard to obtain cumulative hazard

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (\alpha + \beta u)\, du$$

Breaking the integral into two parts:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \alpha\, du + \int_{0}^{t} \beta u\, du$$

Evaluating each:

$$H(t) = \alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate to get the PDF

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}\right]$$

Using the chain rule:

$$f(t) = -e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)} \cdot \left(-\left(\alpha + \beta t\right)\right)$$

$$\boxed{f(t) = (\alpha + \beta t)\, e^{-\left(\alpha t + \frac{\beta t^2}{2}\right)}}$$

### Step 4 — Verify $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$

This is non-trivial for LIH, and is verified using the substitution $u = \sqrt{\beta/2}\, t + \alpha / \sqrt{2\beta}$, leading to:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} (\alpha + \beta t)\, e^{-(\alpha t + \beta t^2/2)}\, dt = 1$$

(since $f(t)$ is a valid PDF by construction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Weibull PDF from First Principles

We use the **Weibull hazard postulate**:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}, \quad t \geq 0$$

### Step 1 — Compute cumulative hazard

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{u}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} du$$

Let $v = u/\eta \Rightarrow u = \eta v,\ du = \eta\, dv$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t/\eta} \frac{\beta}{\eta} v^{\beta - 1} \cdot \eta\, dv = \beta \int_{0}^{t/\eta} v^{\beta - 1}\, dv$$

$$H(t) = \beta \cdot \left[\frac{v^{\beta}}{\beta}\right]_{0}^{t/\eta} = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

### Step 2 — Compute reliability

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate to obtain PDF

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\right]$$

Let $w = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, then $\frac{dw}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$:

$$f(t) = -e^{-w} \cdot \left(-\frac{dw}{dt}\right) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

$$\boxed{f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$

### Step 4 — Special case: Verify it reduces to Exponential when $\beta = 1$

Substituting $\beta = 1$:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{\eta} e^{-t/\eta}$$

This is exactly the **Exponential distribution** with mean $\eta$ — confirming the Weibull generalises the Exponential.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Weibull Distribution

### Step 1 — Mean (MTTF)

$$\mu = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt$$

Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $t = \eta u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{1/\beta - 1} du$:

$$\mu = \int_{0}^{\infty} \eta u^{1/\beta} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta} u^{1 - 1/\beta} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{1/\beta - 1} du$$

Simplifying the powers of $u$:

$$u^{1/\beta} \cdot u^{1 - 1/\beta} \cdot u^{1/\beta - 1} = u^{(1/\beta + 1 - 1/\beta + 1/\beta - 1)} = u^{1/\beta}$$

$$\mu = \eta \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} e^{-u} du = \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

### Step 2 — Second moment $E[T^2]$

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 f(t)\, dt = \eta^2 \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{2/\beta} e^{-u}\, du = \eta^2\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Variance

$$\sigma^2 = E[T^2] - \mu^2 = \eta^2\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \eta^2\, \Gamma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \eta^2\left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical verification for $\beta = 2$ (Rayleigh)

Using $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$ and $\Gamma(2) = 1$:

$$\mu = \eta \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862\,\eta$$

$$\sigma^2 = \eta^2\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)^2\right] = \eta^2\left[1 - \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \approx 0.2146\,\eta^2$$

$$\sigma \approx 0.4633\,\eta$$

These values match standard Rayleigh distribution tables.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

**Problem:** A system has linearly increasing hazard $\lambda(t) = 0.02 + 0.005 t$ (per hour). Find:
1. The reliability at $t = 100$ hours.
2. The probability of failure between $t = 50$ and $t = 100$ hours.
3. The MTTF (use the standard normal table where $\Phi(1.414) \approx 0.921$).

### Solution

**Part 1 — Reliability at $t = 100$ h:**

$$H(100) = 0.02(100) + \frac{0.005 \cdot 100^2}{2} = 2 + 25 = 27$$

$$R(100) = e^{-27} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-12}$$

The reliability is essentially zero — the system is virtually certain to fail within 100 hours.

**Part 2 — Probability of failure in [50, 100]:**

$$P(50 \leq T \leq 100) = F(100) - F(50) = R(50) - R(100)$$

Computing $H(50)$:

$$H(50) = 0.02(50) + \frac{0.005 \cdot 50^2}{2} = 1 + 6.25 = 7.25$$

$$R(50) = e^{-7.25} \approx 7.1 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(50 \leq T \leq 100) \approx 7.1 \times 10^{-4} - 1.88 \times 10^{-12} \approx 7.1 \times 10^{-4}$$

So approximately **0.071%** probability of failure in this window.

**Part 3 — MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}\, e^{\alpha^2 / (2\beta)} \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)$$

Substituting $\alpha = 0.02$, $\beta = 0.005$:

$$\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2\beta}} = \frac{0.02}{\sqrt{0.01}} = \frac{0.02}{0.1} = 0.2$$

$$\frac{\alpha^2}{2\beta} = \frac{0.0004}{0.01} = 0.04$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{0.01}}\, e^{0.04} \cdot \text{erfc}(0.2) = \sqrt{314.159}\, \cdot 1.0408 \cdot \text{erfc}(0.2)$$

$$\text{MTTF} \approx 17.72 \cdot 1.0408 \cdot 0.7773 \approx 14.33 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification using approximation:** For small $\alpha/\sqrt{2\beta}$, $\text{erfc}(z) \approx 1 - \frac{2z}{\sqrt{\pi}}$, giving $\text{erfc}(0.2) \approx 1 - 0.2259 = 0.7741$, consistent with our value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reliability Analysis

The following block diagram shows the interrelationships between hazard, reliability, CDF, and PDF in reliability modelling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazard Rate Function lambda of t"] --> B["Cumulative Hazard H of t equals integral of lambda dt"]
    B --> C["Reliability Function R of t equals exp of minus H of t"]
    C --> D["CDF F of t equals 1 minus R of t"]
    C --> E["PDF f of t equals minus dR by dt"]
    B --> F["MTTF equals integral of R of t dt from 0 to infinity"]
    A --> G["LIH Model lambda equals alpha plus beta t"]
    A --> H["Weibull Model lambda equals beta over eta times t over eta to power beta minus 1"]
    A --> I["Exponential Model lambda equals constant"]
    A --> J["Rayleigh Model lambda equals beta t"]
    G --> K["Three Parameter Weibull Equivalent Form"]
    H --> K
    I --> L["Special Case beta equals 1"]
    J --> L
    L --> M["Choice of Distribution Decision Matrix"]
    K --> M
    M --> N["Infant Mortality beta less than 1"]
    M --> O["Random Failures beta equals 1"]
    M --> P["Wear Out beta greater than 1"]
    F --> Q["Variance and Standard Deviation Computation"]
    C --> Q
    E --> Q
    Q --> R["Reliability Metrics Reported to Engineer"]
```

## 4.2 Distribution Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Failure Data Available"] --> q1{"Hazard rate is constant?"}
    q1 -- yes --> exp["Use Exponential Distribution"]
    q1 -- no --> q2{"Hazard increases with time?"}
    q2 -- yes slowly --> weibullA["Weibull beta between 2 and 4"]
    q2 -- yes linearly --> lih["LIH or Rayleigh Model"]
    q2 -- no --> q3{"Hazard decreases with time?"}
    q3 -- yes --> weibullB["Weibull beta less than 1"]
    q3 -- no --> q4{"Data is repair times skewed?"}
    q4 -- yes --> logn["Lognormal Distribution"]
    q4 -- no --> q5{"Strength or tolerance data?"}
    q5 -- yes --> norm["Normal Distribution"]
    q5 -- no --> q6{"Extreme load or stress values?"}
    q6 -- yes --> gum["Gumbel Extreme Value"]
    q6 -- no --> weibullC["Default to Weibull flexible choice"]
    exp --> report["Compute MTTF Variance Reliability"]
    weibullA --> report
    lih --> report
    weibullB --> report
    logn --> report
    norm --> report
    gum --> report
    weibullC --> report
```

## 4.3 Weibull Shape Parameter Behaviour Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["beta less than 1: Decreasing Hazard"] --> b["Infant Mortality Phase"]
    c["beta equals 1: Constant Hazard"] --> d["Useful Life Random Failures"]
    e["beta greater than 1: Increasing Hazard"] --> f["Wear Out Phase"]
    a -.-> g["PDF Skewed right maximum near origin"]
    c -.-> h["PDF Exponential decay from origin"]
    e -.-> i["PDF Bell shaped shifts right as beta increases"]
    g --> j["Examples Defective electronics manufacturing errors"]
    h --> k["Examples Memory chips mature systems"]
    i --> l["Examples Bearings fatigue mechanical seals"]
```

## 4.4 Component-to-Distribution Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    comp["Engineering Component"] --> type{"Type of failure?"}
    type -- wear out --> mech["Mechanical Bearings Pumps Gears"]
    mech --> wdist["Weibull beta between 2 and 3"]
    type -- fatigue --> fatigue["Aircraft structures Pressure vessels"]
    fatigue --> wdist
    type -- random --> elec["Electronic components Resistors Diodes"]
    elec --> edist["Exponential Constant hazard"]
    type -- infant --> new["Newly manufactured Lot with defects"]
    new --> bdist["Weibull beta less than 1"]
    type -- repair --> rep["Repair time of failed equipment"]
    rep --> ldist["Lognormal"]
    type -- strength --> mat["Material strength Tolerances"]
    mat --> ndist["Normal"]
    wdist --> final["Apply Reliability Analysis"]
    edist --> final
    bdist --> final
    ldist --> final
    ndist --> final
```
