# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Intrinsic Availability from Renewal Theory

**Statement:** For a single-unit repairable system with exponential time-to-failure (constant rate $\lambda$) and exponential time-to-repair (constant rate $\mu$), the steady-state *intrinsic availability* is:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define a renewal cycle.** A renewal cycle is the sequence: *operating until failure → repair → operating until next failure → repair → ...* The cycle has length:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of steady-state availability.** In the *long run*, the fraction of time the system is "up" is the average uptime per cycle divided by the average cycle length:

$$A_i = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{up},i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{up},i} + T_{\text{down},i})} = \frac{E[T_{\text{up}}]}{E[T_{\text{up}}] + E[T_{\text{down}}]}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute means.**

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of rates.** Since for the exponential distribution $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$:

$$A_i = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\mu + \lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Take complement for unavailability.**

$$U = 1 - A_i = \frac{\lambda}{\mu + \lambda} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \approx \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR} \quad \text{(for } \lambda \ll \mu\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The approximation $U \approx \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}$ is the *single most-used result* in reliability engineering trade-off studies and risk-based inspection. Memorise it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Life-Cycle Cost as a Function of $\lambda$ and MTTR

**Statement:** Show that the LCC of a repairable system is a quadratic-like function of $\lambda$, with a unique minimum at the optimal $\lambda^*$.

**Setup.** Let the mission time be $T$ (fixed). Let:
- $C_1$ = acquisition cost, **linearly increasing** with $\lambda_0 - \lambda$ (i.e., *decreasing* in $\lambda$): $C_{\text{ACQ}} = a - b \lambda$, with $a, b > 0$. (A simplified engineering model; in practice the relationship is exponential, but linearisation near the operating point is the standard KTU assumption.)
- $C_{\text{CM}}$ = cost per failure (constant).
- $C_{\text{DT}}$ = downtime cost per hour (constant).
- $C_{\text{PM}}, n_{\text{PM}}$ = PM cost and number, treated as constant for the duration of the design comparison.

**Step 1 — Write LCC.**

$$C_{\text{LCC}}(\lambda, \text{MTTR}) = \underbrace{(a - b\lambda)}_{\text{Acquisition}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{OP}}}_{\text{Operating}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{PM}} n_{\text{PM}}}_{\text{Preventive Maint.}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{CM}} \lambda T}_{\text{Corrective maint.}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{DT}} \lambda T \cdot \text{MTTR}}_{\text{Downtime cost}}$$

**Step 2 — Group terms by powers of $\lambda$.**

$$C_{\text{LCC}} = \underbrace{(a + C_{\text{OP}} + C_{\text{PM}} n_{\text{PM}})}_{\text{constant}} + \underbrace{(-b + C_{\text{CM}} T + C_{\text{DT}} T \cdot \text{MTTR})}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Identify the optimum.** If the slope is *negative*, $C_{\text{LCC}}$ is *decreasing* in $\lambda$ — make $\lambda$ as small as possible (push reliability). If the slope is *positive*, $C_{\text{LCC}}$ is *increasing* in $\lambda$ — make $\lambda$ as large as possible (push cost down).

**Step 4 — Recognise the trade-off boundary.** The break-even $\lambda$ where slope = 0 is:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{b}{C_{\text{CM}} T + C_{\text{DT}} T \cdot \text{MTTR}} = \frac{b}{T(C_{\text{CM}} + C_{\text{DT}} \cdot \text{MTTR})}$$

- If $\lambda > \lambda^*$ → too unreliable, save cost by *increasing* reliability.
- If $\lambda < \lambda^*$ → over-engineered, save cost by accepting slightly higher $\lambda$.

**Step 5 — For a more realistic (convex) acquisition model**, use $C_{\text{ACQ}}(\lambda) = A \cdot e^{k(R_0 - R)} = A \cdot e^{k(1 - e^{-\lambda T} - R_0)}$, which makes the equation transcendental and is solved numerically. KTU exams typically allow the linear model.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimal Preventive Maintenance Interval (Age Replacement)

**Setup.** A non-repairable component has increasing failure rate $h(t)$ (Weibull, $h(t) = \beta t^{\beta-1}/\eta^\beta$). We replace it either at failure (cost $C_f$) or at scheduled age $T_p$ (cost $C_p < C_f$). Find $T_p^*$.

**Step 1 — Long-run cost per unit time (Renewal Reward Theorem).**

$$C(T_p) = \frac{C_p \cdot S(T_p) + C_f \cdot F(T_p)}{T_p \cdot S(T_p) + \int_0^{T_p} t \, f(t) \, dt}$$

Where $S(T_p) = R(T_p)$ = survival probability, $F(T_p) = 1 - R(T_p)$ = failure probability.

**Step 2 — Take derivative, set to zero.**

$$\frac{dC}{dT_p} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_p = T_p^*$$

For Weibull, the equation is transcendental and tabulated (Barlow–Hunter optimal $T_p$ is approximately $0.7 \times$ mean life when $C_p / C_f \approx 0.5$).

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**
- If $C_f \gg C_p$ (catastrophic failure cost) → $T_p$ is short (replace often, prevent failures).
- If $C_f \approx C_p$ → $T_p$ is long or infinite (replace only on failure).
- This is the **preventive-vs-corrective** sub-trade-off inside the broader R–M trade-off.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A machine has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$/hr and a constant repair rate $\mu = 0.5$/hr. The acquisition cost increases with reliability as $C_{\text{ACQ}} = 50{,}000 + 200{,}000 \cdot (\lambda_0 - \lambda)$ where $\lambda_0 = 0.002$/hr is the baseline. Operating cost $C_{\text{OP}} = 10{,}000$/yr, $C_{\text{CM}} = 2{,}000$ per failure, $C_{\text{DT}} = 500$/hr, mission time $T = 2000$ hr, no preventive maintenance.

**Step 1 — Find baseline LCC at $\lambda = 0.001$/hr.**

$$C_{\text{ACQ}} = 50{,}000 + 200{,}000 \cdot (0.002 - 0.001) = 50{,}000 + 200 = 50{,}200$$

Number of failures: $n_f = \lambda T = 0.001 \times 2000 = 2$ failures.

Repair rate gives MTTR: $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu = 1/0.5 = 2$ hr.

Total downtime: $T_D = n_f \cdot \text{MTTR} = 2 \times 2 = 4$ hr.

$$C_{\text{LCC}}(\lambda=0.001) = 50{,}200 + 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 \times 2 + 500 \times 4 = 50{,}200 + 10{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 66{,}200$$

**Step 2 — Try improved design with $\lambda = 0.0005$/hr (MTTR unchanged at 2 hr).**

$$C_{\text{ACQ}} = 50{,}000 + 200{,}000 \cdot (0.002 - 0.0005) = 50{,}000 + 300 = 50{,}300$$

$n_f = 0.0005 \times 2000 = 1$ failure. $T_D = 1 \times 2 = 2$ hr.

$$C_{\text{LCC}} = 50{,}300 + 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 \times 1 + 500 \times 2 = 50{,}300 + 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 63{,}300$$

**Step 3 — Try $\lambda = 0.0001$/hr.**

$$C_{\text{ACQ}} = 50{,}000 + 200{,}000 \cdot (0.002 - 0.0001) = 50{,}000 + 380 = 50{,}380$$

$n_f = 0.0001 \times 2000 = 0.2$ failure. $T_D = 0.2 \times 2 = 0.4$ hr.

$$C_{\text{LCC}} = 50{,}380 + 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 \times 0.2 + 500 \times 0.4 = 50{,}380 + 10{,}000 + 400 + 200 = 60{,}980$$

**Step 4 — Find optimal $\lambda^*$ from the linear formula.**

$$\lambda^* = \frac{b}{T(C_{\text{CM}} + C_{\text{DT}} \cdot \text{MTTR})} = \frac{200{,}000}{2000 \times (2{,}000 + 500 \times 2)} = \frac{200{,}000}{2000 \times 3{,}000} = \frac{200{,}000}{6{,}000{,}000} = 0.0333 \text{ /hr}$$

This is unphysically large, telling us that the *minimum* of the LCC curve lies at a $\lambda$ *smaller* than any value we tested, and the acquisition-cost linearisation slope $b$ should be re-estimated.

**Step 5 — Engineering takeaway.** The trade-off curve is *flat* near the optimum — wide region of near-optimal designs. The exam usually expects the student to (a) set up the LCC equation, (b) compute the LCC at 2–3 candidate design points, and (c) *recommend the lowest-LCC option* with a clear cost-breakdown statement.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic

| If the dominant cost is … | Then the design should push … | Trade-off direction |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{ACQ}}$ (capital budget crunch) | Higher $\lambda$ (lower R) and moderate MTTR | Skewed toward *M* |
| $C_{\text{CM}}$ (repairs expensive) | Lower $\lambda$ (higher R) and lower MTTR | Skewed toward *R and M both* |
| $C_{\text{DT}}$ (downtime cripples revenue) | Aggressively low MTTR, then R as budget allows | Skewed toward *M* first, then *R* |
| Safety / regulatory | Drive R to maximum affordable; M secondary | Skewed toward *R* |
| $C_{\text{PM}}$ (spares & labour heavy) | Higher $\lambda$, run-to-failure | Skewed toward *no preventive* |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reliability–Maintainability Trade-off Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[System Design Requirement] --> B[Define Mission Profile and Time T]
    B --> C[Estimate Cost Parameters]
    C --> C1[Acquisition Cost C ACQ]
    C --> C2[Operating Cost C OP]
    C --> C3[Corrective Cost C CM]
    C --> C4[Downtime Cost C DT per hour]
    C --> C5[PM Cost C PM]
    C1 --> D[Build Life Cycle Cost LCC Equation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[LCC as function of lambda and MTTR]
    E --> F{Compute Availability A i}
    F --> G[A i equals MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
    G --> H{Meets Availability Target A target?}
    H -->|No| I[Identify Binding Constraint]
    I --> I1[Reliability too low: increase C ACQ]
    I --> I2[Maintainability too low: improve MTTR via BIT and training]
    I1 --> J[Re iterate LCC and Availability]
    I2 --> J
    H -->|Yes| K[Check LCC is within Budget]
    K -->|No| J
    K -->|Yes| L[Final Design Locked]
    L --> M[Monitor Field Performance]
    M --> N[Update lambda and MTTR Estimates]
    N --> C
```

## 4.2 Cost Trade-off Architecture (R-Side and M-Side Curves)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph C_CURVES[Cost versus Design Variable]
        AX[Design Variable: Reliability R or MTTR] --> C1[Acquisition Cost Curve: Monotonically Rising]
        AX --> C2[Failure plus Downtime Cost Curve: Monotonically Falling]
        AX --> C3[Total LCC Curve: U Shaped]
    end
    C1 --> SUM[Add at each x]
    C2 --> SUM
    SUM --> C3
    C3 --> OPT[Locate Minimum: Optimal R star or MTTR star]
    OPT --> DEC[Select Design Point]

    SENS --> DEC
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER[Input Layer]
        REQ[Mission Requirements]
        COST[Cost Constraints]

    end

    COST --> RM

    subgraph ANALYSIS_LAYER[Analysis Layer]

    end

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test *recall* and *conceptual clarity*. Answers should be 3–5 sentences with at least one formula. Avoid essay-style answers.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Define the Reliability–Maintainability (R–M) trade-off. Why is it necessary to study this trade-off during the design phase rather than the operational phase?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Reliability–Maintainability (R–M) trade-off is the engineering process of finding the **optimal balance** between the reliability of a system (quantified by MTBF) and its maintainability (quantified by MTTR), such that the **total life-cycle cost is minimised** and the **operational availability is maximised** within given constraints. It is studied during the *design phase* because **70–90% of a system's life-cycle cost is committed by design decisions** — once a system is operational, only the *maintenance policy* can be changed, not the *intrinsic* MTBF and MTTR. Fixing the trade-off post-design is therefore 5–10× more expensive than optimising it at the design stage. **Key formula:** $A_i = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Explain with a sketch how the total life-cycle cost (LCC) of a system varies with reliability. Indicate the optimal point.**

**Model Answer:**

> The total LCC of a system, when plotted as a function of reliability $R$ (or its complement, failure rate $\lambda$), has a **characteristic U-shape**. As $R$ increases, the **acquisition cost $C_{\text{ACQ}}$ rises** (better components, redundancy, derating) and the **failure-and-downtime cost falls** (fewer corrective actions, less lost production). The sum — the LCC — has a unique minimum at the **optimal reliability $R^*$**. For $R < R^*$, the system is *under-designed* (fails too often); for $R > R^*$, it is *over-engineered* (costs more to build than it saves in operation). The optimal point satisfies:
> $$\frac{d C_{\text{LCC}}}{d R} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{d C_{\text{LCC}}}{d \lambda} = 0$$
> The LCC curve is **flat near the optimum** — a wide band of near-optimal designs exists, which is robust against parameter uncertainty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #1):** The most common mistake students make in this question is to *only* write the availability equation $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ and stop there. The Board Examiner expects a **Cost Equation**, an **Availability Equation**, **2–3 design-point calculations**, and a **clear recommendation**. Writing only the formula without numbers costs 5–7 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #2):** Students often confuse **MTBF** (for repairable) with **MTTF** (for non-repairable). The question will specify. Marks are deducted for this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

A repairable industrial pump has the following characteristics under the *current* design:

- Failure rate: $\lambda = 0.02$ failures per hour.
- Repair rate: $\mu = 0.25$ repairs per hour.
- Mission time: $T = 1000$ hours.
- Cost per failure (corrective maintenance): $C_{\text{CM}} = \text{Rs. } 15{,}000$.
- Downtime cost: $C_{\text{DT}} = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000$ per hour.
- Acquisition cost of *current* design: $C_{\text{ACQ,0}} = \text{Rs. } 8{,}00{,}000$.
- Acquisition cost increases by Rs. 1,00,000 for every 0.005/hr reduction in $\lambda$ (i.e., $C_{\text{ACQ}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.02 - \lambda}{0.005}$).
- MTTR is unchanged by redesign at $1/\mu = 4$ hours.
- Operating cost and preventive maintenance cost are constant and ignored.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the intrinsic availability, expected number of failures, total downtime cost, and total life-cycle cost for the current design.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Evaluate the LCC for two improved designs: Design 1 with $\lambda_1 = 0.015$/hr and Design 2 with $\lambda_2 = 0.010$/hr. Recommend the optimal design and justify with the R–M trade-off principle.**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Intrinsic Availability.**

$$A_i = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{1/0.02}{1/0.02 + 1/0.25} = \frac{50}{50 + 4} = \frac{50}{54} = 0.9259$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of failures in mission time $T = 1000$ hr.**

$$n_f = \lambda T = 0.02 \times 1000 = 20 \text{ failures}$$

**Step 3 — MTTR and total downtime.**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

$$T_D = n_f \times \text{MTTR} = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Total downtime cost.**

$$C_{\text{DT,total}} = C_{\text{DT}} \times T_D = 2{,}000 \times 80 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Corrective maintenance cost.**

$$C_{\text{CM,total}} = C_{\text{CM}} \times n_f = 15{,}000 \times 20 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Total life-cycle cost.**

$$C_{\text{LCC}} = C_{\text{ACQ}} + C_{\text{CM,total}} + C_{\text{DT,total}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 12{,}60{,}000$$

**Answer (a):** $A_i = 0.9259$; $n_f = 20$; $T_D = 80$ hr; $C_{\text{LCC}} = \text{Rs. } 12{,}60{,}000$.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Design 1: $\lambda_1 = 0.015$/hr**

$$C_{\text{ACQ,1}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.02 - 0.015}{0.005} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 9{,}00{,}000$$

$$n_{f,1} = 0.015 \times 1000 = 15 \text{ failures}$$

$$T_{D,1} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ hours}$$

$$C_{\text{DT,1}} = 2{,}000 \times 60 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{CM,1}} = 15{,}000 \times 15 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}25{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{LCC,1}} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}25{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 12{,}45{,}000$$

**Design 2: $\lambda_2 = 0.010$/hr**

$$C_{\text{ACQ,2}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.02 - 0.010}{0.005} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$n_{f,2} = 0.010 \times 1000 = 10 \text{ failures}$$

$$T_{D,2} = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ hours}$$

$$C_{\text{DT,2}} = 2{,}000 \times 40 = \text{Rs. } 80{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{CM,2}} = 15{,}000 \times 10 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{LCC,2}} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 12{,}30{,}000$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Design | $\lambda$ (1/hr) | $C_{\text{ACQ}}$ (Rs.) | $C_{\text{CM}}$ (Rs.) | $C_{\text{DT}}$ (Rs.) | $C_{\text{LCC}}$ (Rs.) | $A_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current | 0.020 | 8,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 1,60,000 | 12,60,000 | 0.9259 |
| Design 1 | 0.015 | 9,00,000 | 2,25,000 | 1,20,000 | 12,45,000 | 0.9375 |
| Design 2 | 0.010 | 10,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 80,000 | **12,30,000** | 0.9615 |

**Recommendation:** **Design 2 is optimal** — it gives the **lowest LCC (Rs. 12,30,000)** and the **highest availability (96.15%)**. The extra Rs. 2,00,000 spent on acquisition is more than offset by the Rs. 2,30,000 saved in corrective and downtime costs. The trade-off principle is satisfied: *the marginal cost of improving reliability (Rs. 1,00,000 per 0.005/hr reduction) is less than the marginal benefit (Rs. 1,15,000 in operating-cost savings per 0.005/hr reduction).* This confirms the U-shaped LCC principle — the current design is *under-designed* and the design should be pushed further toward higher R until the curve turns up.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

A non-repairable electronic module in a satellite has the following design alternatives. The mission duration is $T = 5$ years ($= 43{,}800$ hours, assume continuous operation). The cost parameters are:

- Failure cost (loss of mission, including replacement launch): $C_f = \text{Rs. } 50$ crore.
- Preventive replacement cost (planned service): $C_p = \text{Rs. } 2$ crore.
- Acquisition cost of a higher-reliability design (design B) is Rs. 3 crore more than the baseline (design A).

The module's time-to-failure follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta = 2$ and characteristic life $\eta = 60{,}000$ hours (Design A) or $\eta = 90{,}000$ hours (Design B). The failure rate function is $h(t) = \beta t^{\beta-1} / \eta^\beta$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**For Design A, compute the reliability at mission end ($t = 5$ years), the mean time to failure (MTTF), and the expected mission-failure cost (probability of failure × $C_f$).**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Design A and Design B from a reliability–maintainability (in this case preventive-replacement) trade-off viewpoint. Recommend the better design using total expected cost over the mission.**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Reliability of Design A at $t = T = 43{,}800$ hr.**

The Weibull reliability function is:

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

For Design A with $\eta = 60{,}000$ hr, $\beta = 2$:

$$R_A(43800) = e^{-(43800/60000)^2} = e^{-(0.7300)^2} = e^{-0.5329} = 0.5869$$

So the **probability of survival over 5 years ≈ 58.7%**, and **probability of failure** $F_A = 1 - 0.5869 = 0.4131$ (41.3%).

**Step 2 — MTTF of Weibull distribution.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

For $\beta = 2$: $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$.

$$\text{MTTF}_A = 60{,}000 \times 0.8862 = 53{,}172 \text{ hours} \approx 6.07 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — Expected mission-failure cost.**

$$E[C_f] = C_f \cdot F_A = 50 \times 0.4131 = \text{Rs. } 20.66 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 4 — Add acquisition cost (Design A baseline).**

Let $C_{\text{ACQ,A}} = 0$ (baseline). Total expected cost of Design A over the mission:

$$E[C_{\text{total,A}}] = C_{\text{ACQ,A}} + E[C_f] = 0 + 20.66 = \text{Rs. } 20.66 \text{ crore}$$

**Answer (a):** $R_A(5\text{ yr}) = 0.5869$; $\text{MTTF}_A = 53{,}172$ hr; Expected mission-failure cost = Rs. 20.66 crore.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Reliability of Design B at $t = 43{,}800$ hr** (with $\eta = 90{,}000$ hr, $\beta = 2$):

$$R_B(43800) = e^{-(43800/90000)^2} = e^{-(0.4867)^2} = e^{-0.2369} = 0.7891$$

So **probability of failure** $F_B = 1 - 0.7891 = 0.2109$ (21.1%).

**Step 2 — Expected mission-failure cost for Design B.**

$$E[C_f]_B = C_f \cdot F_B = 50 \times 0.2109 = \text{Rs. } 10.55 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 3 — Total expected cost of Design B.**

$$E[C_{\text{total,B}}] = C_{\text{ACQ,B}} + E[C_f]_B = 3 + 10.55 = \text{Rs. } 13.55 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 4 — Include preventive-replacement option for Design A.** If a preventive replacement at $t_p = 30{,}000$ hr is performed on Design A:

$$R_A(30000) = e^{-(30000/60000)^2} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788$$

Probability of surviving the *first leg* (0 to 30,000 hr) = 0.7788. Then a fresh module of the same Weibull distribution operates from 30,000 to 43,800 hr (i.e., remaining 13,800 hr):

$$R_A(13800) = e^{-(13800/60000)^2} = e^{-0.0529} = 0.9485$$

Probability of *also* surviving the second leg = 0.9485. Total probability of *no* failure with one PM:

$$P_{\text{no failure}} = 0.7788 \times 0.9485 = 0.7387$$

Probability of *at least one failure* = $1 - 0.7387 = 0.2613$.

Total expected cost of Design A with one PM at $t_p = 30{,}000$ hr:

$$E[C_{\text{total, A+PM}}] = 0 + C_p + C_f \cdot 0.2613 = 2 + 50 \times 0.2613 = 2 + 13.07 = \text{Rs. } 15.07 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Table:**

| Strategy | Acquisition (Rs. cr) | PM Cost (Rs. cr) | Expected Failure Cost (Rs. cr) | Total Expected Cost (Rs. cr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Design A, no PM | 0 | 0 | 20.66 | 20.66 |
| Design A + 1 PM at 30k hr | 0 | 2.00 | 13.07 | 15.07 |
| Design B, no PM | 3.00 | 0 | 10.55 | **13.55** |

**Recommendation:** **Design B without preventive maintenance** is optimal, with a total expected cost of **Rs. 13.55 crore** — lower than Design A (with or without PM) by Rs. 1.5–7 crore. The trade-off rationale: spending Rs. 3 crore on a higher-reliability design B reduces the expected failure cost by Rs. 10.11 crore (from 20.66 to 10.55), a net saving of Rs. 7.11 crore. The R–M trade-off here favours the **reliability side (higher $\eta$)** over the **maintainability side (preventive replacement)**, because the failure cost is so high (Rs. 50 cr) that *any* probability reduction is worth significant investment.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Triggers on R–M Trade-off Questions (from KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the acquisition-cost term** $C_{\text{ACQ}}$ in the LCC. Many students only sum failure + downtime costs. Marks: –3.
> 2. **Mixing up MTTR (repairable) with MTTF (non-repairable).** If the question says "non-repairable module" or "throwaway component", you must use MTTF, not MTTR. Marks: –2.
> 3. **Not stating the units** of $\lambda$ (1/hr), MTTR (hr), cost (Rs.). Examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark for "units & assumptions stated". Marks: –1.
> 4. **No recommendation / no justification.** Computing three LCC values without saying *which design is best and why* loses 2–3 marks in the final "discussion" part.
> 5. **Ignoring the U-shape principle.** A student who recommends "the design with the highest reliability" without checking LCC is missing the core point of the trade-off.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this to your wall before the exam):**
> - The R–M trade-off aims to **minimise Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** while **maximising Availability (A)**.
> - The core coupling equation is $A_i = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — a single scalar that fuses R and M.
> - The unavailability approximation $U \approx \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}$ is the workhorse of safety analyses.
> - LCC has three components: $C_{\text{ACQ}}$ (rises with R) + $C_{\text{OP}}$ (constant) + $C_{\text{CM}} + C_{\text{DT}}$ (fall with R).
> - The LCC-vs-R curve is **U-shaped**; the minimum defines the **optimal $R^*$**.
> - For **repairable** systems use **MTBF / MTTR**; for **non-repairable** use **MTTF**.
> - Decision rule: push reliability while $\frac{d C_{\text{ACQ}}}{dR} < \frac{d C_{\text{failure+downtime}}}{dR}$; stop when they equal.
> - Preventive maintenance (PM) is justified only if $C_f > C_p$ and failure is non-random (increasing $h(t)$).
> - The **rule of thumb**: 70–90% of LCC is committed at the design phase — trade-off must be done *before* construction.
> - In exam answers, always end with: *"The recommended design is … because its LCC of Rs. … is the minimum among the candidates, and its availability of … % meets the target."*
> - For exponential distributions: $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$, $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$.
> - Always **tabulate** the comparison of alternatives — examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a clean, labelled table.
